### `docs/architecture/BILLING_AND_TIERS.md`
```md
# Billing, Tiers & FinOps Strategy
This document outlines the SaaS tiering strategy, operational limits, and Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG) FinOps calculations across all modules of the Frugal Fortress.
## 1. Global Tiering Philosophy
The system enforces a strict separation between Free and Paid compute stacks to protect profit margins and ensure enterprise-grade privacy.
*   **Free Tier:** Utilizes third-party APIs (e.g., LlamaParse, Groq Llama-3.3). Data may be subject to standard API retention policies. Strictly rate-limited.
*   **Paid Tiers (Premium, Pro, PAYG):** Exclusively utilizes Google Vertex AI (Gemini 2.5 Flash/Pro). Guarantees **Zero Data Retention** (SOC2/GDPR compliant).
## 2. Module-Specific Limits & Margins
### A. Receipt Parser
*   **Free:** 30 receipts/month. Max 100 stored. (Stack: LlamaParse + Groq).
*   **Premium ($2.99/mo):** 500 receipts/month. Max 2,000 stored. (Stack: Vertex Flash-Lite).
    *   *FinOps:* Max cost per receipt (with 3 retries) = $0.0009. Max monthly cost = $0.46. **Margin: 84%**.
*   **Pro ($9.99/mo):** 2,000 receipts/month. Max 5,000 stored. (Stack: Vertex Flash).
    *   *FinOps:* Max cost per receipt (with 3 retries) = $0.0047. Max monthly cost = $9.51. **Margin: ~5%** (Loss-leader / High-volume tier).
### B. PDF to Anki
*   **Free:** 50 pages/month. Max 10 pages/PDF. Max 100 flashcards.
*   **Premium ($2.99/mo):** 1,000 pages/month. Max 100 pages/PDF. Max 5,000 flashcards.
    *   *FinOps:* Max cost per 100-page PDF = $0.07. Max monthly cost (10 PDFs) = $0.70. **Margin: 76%**.
*   **Pro ($4.99/mo):** 2,000 pages/month. Max 500 pages/PDF. Max 20,000 flashcards.
    *   *FinOps:* Max cost per 500-page PDF = $0.36. Max monthly cost (4 PDFs) = $1.47. **Margin: 70%**.
### C. AURA
*   **Free:** 150 mins/month. Max 5 mins/audio. (Stack: Groq Whisper + Llama-3.3).
*   **Premium ($4.99/mo):** 3,000 mins/month. Max 20 mins/audio. (Stack: Vertex Flash-Lite Multimodal).
*   **Pro ($9.99/mo):** 9,000 mins/month. Max 45 mins/audio. (Stack: Vertex Flash-Lite Multimodal).
## 3. Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG) Wallet Architecture
For enterprise users exceeding Pro limits, we implement a prepaid wallet system.
*   **Formula:** `(Raw LLM Provider Cost) * 1.40` (40% Markup).
*   **Mechanism:** The `BudgetManager` uses atomic Lua scripts in Redis to reserve funds before inference and commit the exact cost post-inference.
*   **Model Selection:** PAYG users can dynamically select their preferred Vertex AI model (e.g., upgrading from Flash to Pro for complex PDFs). The system dynamically calculates the maximum allowed context window based on their current USD balance.
## 4. Data Lifecycle & Churn (Right to be Forgotten)
When a user cancels their subscription via Stripe:
1.  **Grace Period (Days 1-90):** The user is downgraded to Free Tier compute limits, but their historical data (flashcards, receipts, glossary rules) remains intact and exportable.
2.  **Hard Pruning (Day 91):** An automated `Arq` cron job executes a FIFO truncation, permanently deleting vector embeddings and database rows that exceed the Free Tier storage limits.
```

### `docs/architecture/security_compliance_soc2.md`
```md
# Security & Compliance Architecture (SOC2 / GDPR / HIPAA)
This document outlines the data lifecycle, encryption standards, and privacy guardrails implemented in the Frugal Fortress architecture to meet strict Enterprise compliance requirements.
## 1. Data Lifecycle & Zero Data Retention
We operate under a strict **Zero-Trust** and **Data Minimization** policy.
1. **Ingestion:** All payloads (PDFs, Images, Audio) are transmitted via HTTPS (TLS 1.3).
2. **Ephemeral Storage:** Raw files are encrypted and stored in Redis with a strict **1-hour TTL**. They are purged immediately after the background worker (`Arq`) processes them.
3. **Inference Processing:**
   *   **Paid Tiers (Premium, Pro, PAYG):** Processed exclusively via Google Vertex AI. We rely on Google Cloud's Enterprise terms, which guarantee **Zero Data Retention** (customer data is NEVER used to train foundational models).
   *   **Free Tier:** Processed via Groq/LlamaParse. Users explicitly consent to standard API terms.
4. **Right to be Forgotten (Pruning):** If a user cancels their subscription, a 90-day grace period begins. On day 91, an automated cron job (`maintain_pdf_data_lifecycle`) permanently deletes all associated vector embeddings, flashcards, and receipts.
## 2. Encryption Standards
### A. Encryption in Transit
All internal (VPC) and external communications are secured via TLS 1.2/1.3.
### B. Application-Level Encryption (ALE)
To protect against database compromises (e.g., a DBA accessing raw tables or a SQL injection), sensitive user data is encrypted *before* it leaves the Python application.
*   **Implementation:** `cryptography.fernet` (AES-128-CBC with HMAC-SHA256).
*   **Target Data:** Glossary Rules, Transcription logs, Flashcard Fronts/Backs, Deck Titles, Receipt Payloads, and User Profile data (Email, Display Name).
*   **Reference:** `src/app/modules/audio_notes/infrastructure/audio_notes_ale.py` and `src/app/security/feedback_privacy.py`.
## 3. Data Loss Prevention (DLP) & PII Scrubbing
Before any text is sent to an external LLM (especially on the Free Tier), it passes through our internal scrubbing pipeline.
*   **Regex Pass:** Deterministic removal of Emails, Phone Numbers, SSNs, and Credit Cards.
*   **NLP Pass (Microsoft Presidio):** Contextual Named Entity Recognition (NER) replaces names and organizations with safe tokens (e.g., `[PERSON]`, `[ORG]`).
*   **Fail-Secure Local SLM (Optional):** A local Llama-3.2-3B model evaluates prompts for complex PHI/PII leaks. If a leak is detected, or if the local model crashes, the request is blocked (`SecurityPolicyViolation`). It fails closed.
## 4. Access Control & Multi-Tenancy
*   **Authentication:** Handled via Google OAuth2 (OpenID Connect).
*   **Module Isolation:** JWTs are strictly scoped per module (`module=pdf_anki` vs `module=receipt_parser`) to prevent lateral movement between product contexts.
*   **Tenant Isolation:** The `ContextMiddleware` binds the `tenant_id` to Python `contextvars`. All database repositories and vector searches (LanceDB/pgvector) enforce a hard `WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id` clause. Cross-tenant data leakage is mathematically impossible at the query level.
```

### `docs/architecture/PERFORMANCE_ARCHITECTURE.md`
```md
# Performance Architecture — Performance Architect Level
This document describes the **performance strategies** implemented and planned for the three flagship modules: **PDF to Anki**, **Receipt Parser**, and **Audio Notes**. It aligns with the "Performance Architect" narrative: system and compute optimization, FinOps, and resilience.
---
## 1. Project 3: PDF to Anki — The Async Knowledge Beast
**Evolution:** From "Upload a PDF" to **Resilient Mass Ingest System**.
### 1.1 Strategy: Async & Queues + Semantic / Exact Hash Caching
| Pain | Solution |
|------|----------|
| "If I upload the BOE (500 pages), your API blows up." | Decoupled architecture: **Arq + Redis**. Upload returns 202; processing runs in a background worker. No long HTTP timeout. |
| Same PDF uploaded twice (e.g. same textbook) | **Exact hash cache**: `file_hash = SHA-256(pdf_bytes)`. If `pdf:deck:{file_hash}` exists in Redis, the worker returns the cached deck in ~50 ms and does not call LlamaParse/LLM. |
**Stack (actual):** FastAPI + **Arq** (not Celery) + Redis. Router: LiteLLM. Vector store: LanceDB (S3/R2).
### 1.2 Implemented Behaviour
- **Async flow:** Upload → store **encrypted** raw bytes in Redis (TTL 1h) → enqueue `process_pdf_task` → worker: load PDF from Redis → decrypt → LlamaParse → chunk → embed → LanceDB → flashcard generation → store **encrypted** deck in Redis (`pdf:deck:{file_hash}`, TTL 7 days).
- **Cache hit L1 (exact hash):** Before parsing, worker calls `cache_get(file_hash)`. If a deck exists for that hash, state is set to COMPLETED and the cached deck is returned; no LlamaParse nor LLM.
- **Semantic dedup L2:** Each new chunk embedding is searched in LanceDB; if a highly similar concept already exists (≥95% similarity), LLM generation for that chunk is skipped (FinOps optimisation).
- **Idempotency:** Duplicate uploads (same hash) are detected in the router via `get_by_file_hash`; client gets the same `task_id` or "already queued".
### 1.3 Token Optimization
- **Goal:** Reduce cost and latency by not sending headers/footers (page numbers, "Page X of Y", repeated titles) to the LLM.
- **Implementation:** Pure helper `strip_headers_footers(text)` in `app.modules.pdf_anki.application.token_optimizer`. Applied to chunk text **before** sending to the flashcard LLM (see `FlashcardGeneratorAdapter`). Embeddings can still use full chunk for better retrieval; only the flashcard prompt sees trimmed text.
### 1.4 Semantic Caching (Phase 1 Implemented)
- **Current:** Exact hash cache (L1) + per-chunk **semantic deduplication** (L2) in `process_pdf_use_case`. This avoids re-paying for repeated explanations across documents.
- **Next:** Deck-level semantic cache (full-document reuse) and tenant-aware keys; may be implemented as a follow-up ADR.
### 1.5 Nested Learning — The Adaptive Teacher (Roadmap)
- **Idea:** User corrects a flashcard → system stores the correction as a positive example (e.g. in LanceDB or a feedback table). Next time we generate cards for that user/topic, inject few-shot examples (e.g. via DSPy BootstrapFewShot or a simple retrieval step) so the model adapts to the user’s style.
- **Status:** Design only; not implemented. Value: "The system learns the user’s pedagogical style; the more they use it, the fewer corrections they need."
### 1.6 Tests
- Unit: `process_pdf_use_case` with `cache_get` returning a deck → status completed, no parser/vector_store calls.
- Unit: `strip_headers_footers` removes common patterns.
- Integration: `test_pdf_resilience`: timeout/error → state FAILED.
- Worker: `process_pdf_task` with mocked Redis cache returning deck → document COMPLETED, deck from cache.
---
## 2. Project 5: Receipt Parser — The Cost-Efficient Fortress
**Architecture:** Hybrid — Edge (OCR) + Cloud (orchestration).
### 2.1 Value Proposition
"This system is not just a receipt reader. It is a **hybrid AI architecture** for maximum privacy and minimum cost: we process vision locally (CPU) to remove image transfer and cloud vision cost; we use **intelligent LLM orchestration** to structure data, achieving high accuracy at a fraction of traditional cost."
### 2.2 Current Implementation (Cloud Orchestration)
- **Input:** Raw OCR text (from Edge or external OCR). We do **not** process images in the backend today; the "compute" layer is at the edge.
- **FinOps — LLM router:**
  - **Level 1 (cheap):** Groq Llama-3.1-8B. Prompt: extract JSON (items, subtotal, tax, total, currency). Cost ~$0.0001 per receipt.
  - **Validation (pure Python):** `validate_receipt_math`: items sum = subtotal, subtotal + tax = total. No framework.
  - **Escalation:** If validation fails after retries (e.g. 2× Llama, then 1× GPT-4o), we call **GPT-4o** with an error-correcting prompt. Only ~5–10% of receipts escalate; cost remains low overall.
### 2.3 Edge / Compute Performance (Roadmap)
- **Goal:** Remove dependency on GPT-4 Vision; keep images on-prem or on Edge.
- **Techniques (when we add image input):**
  - **Preprocessing (OpenCV/NumPy):** Perspective correction (homography), adaptive thresholding (Otsu), noise reduction (morphological ops). Pure math on CPU; improves OCR accuracy.
  - **Local OCR:** PaddleOCR or Tesseract exported to **ONNX**, optionally **quantized (int8)** for 3–4× speed and lower RAM.
  - **Singleton model:** Load ONNX model once at FastAPI startup; reuse for every request.
### 2.4 Railway / Low-Memory Rules (when doing image processing)
- **Downsampling:** Resize image to max 1024 px width before heavy ops (e.g. 100 MB → 10 MB RAM).
- **Singleton:** One ONNX model instance per process.
- **Optional GC:** After processing a large image, `del` reference and `gc.collect()` to free RAM sooner.
### 2.5 Dynamic Few-Shot Retrieval (Roadmap)
- **Idea:** Before calling the LLM, search a vector store for **validated receipts from the same vendor** (e.g. "Ferretería Pepe"). If found, inject 1–2 examples into the prompt. The model then "learns" that vendor’s format without retraining.
- **Status:** Design only; requires vendor extraction + vector index of validated receipts.
### 2.6 Tests
- Unit: `validate_receipt_math` (valid / invalid totals).
- Unit: `run_self_correction_loop`: invalid JSON → error; valid JSON but wrong math → retries then smart model; valid JSON + correct math → success.
- Evaluation: `test_receipt_extraction.py` (DeepEval-style; mock and optional live LLM).
---
## 3. Project 2: Audio Notes — The Smart Listener
**Evolution:** From "Transcribe audio" to **Corporate Memory**.
### 3.1 Strategy: Hash-Based Cache (System Performance)
| Pain | Solution |
|------|----------|
| Five employees upload the same Zoom recording; you pay 5× for transcription and summary. | **Content hash (SHA-256)** of the audio. Key: `audio:{hash}`. If cache hit → return stored summary in ~50 ms; cost $0. |
### 3.2 Implemented Behaviour
- **Flow:** Upload → compute content hash → `idempotency.get_cached(hash)`.
  - **Hit:** Return `status: "cached"` and stored summary; no Whisper, no LLM.
  - **Miss:** Transcribe (Whisper) → summarize (LLM) → store in cache → return.
- **Idempotency:** `RedisIdempotencyGuard` with TTL; same file always yields same result and one processing run.
### 3.3 Tests
- Unit: Idempotency hash stable; get/set cached.
- Integration: Upload same file twice → second request cached.
---
## 4. Native and Accelerated Code (Strategy)
When **pure Python** is the bottleneck (e.g. heavy math, image ops, embedding loops), consider, in order:
1. **Profile first:** Identify hot paths (e.g. with `cProfile` or PySpy).
2. **Python-level:** `asyncio.to_thread()` for CPU-bound work; batch APIs to reduce round-trips.
3. **NumPy/SciPy:** Vectorise; avoid Python loops where possible.
4. **Native / compiled:**
   - **OpenCV (C++):** Already used via Python bindings; for new pipelines, C++ extensions or cv2 is sufficient unless you need custom kernels.
   - **ONNX Runtime:** For inference (OCR, classifiers); int8 quantization for speed and memory.
   - **Rust/PyO3:** For new pure-math or I/O-heavy libraries; call from Python via PyO3.
   - **CUDA/Triton:** For GPU-bound inference or custom kernels (e.g. embedding batches).
   - **WASM:** For client-side or sandboxed compute (e.g. browser); not typically for backend.
5. **Infrastructure:** **Kafka** (or Redis Streams, as we use) for event-driven, back-pressure-safe pipelines; **DB indexing** (e.g. on `file_hash`, `tenant_id`, time) for fast lookups; **load balancers** and horizontal scaling for API and workers.
**Rule:** Prefer Python + NumPy + ONNX for most cases; introduce C++/Rust/CUDA only when profiling shows a clear win and maintenance cost is acceptable. Document the decision in an ADR.
---
## 5. References
- [ARCHITECTURE.md](../ARCHITECTURE.md) — High-level architecture.
- [docs/HEXAGONAL_DECOUPLING.md](HEXAGONAL_DECOUPLING.md) — Layer boundaries.
- [docs/DESGLOSE_IMPLEMENTACION_BEST_PRACTICES.md](DESGLOSE_IMPLEMENTACION_BEST_PRACTICES.md) — Arq, DeepEval, resilience.
- [docs/adr/007-native-acceleration-strategy.md](adr/007-native-acceleration-strategy.md) — When to use ONNX, Rust, CUDA, etc.
```

### `docs/architecture/API_VERSIONING_STRATEGY.md`
```md
# API Versioning & Deprecation Strategy
To ensure stability for our frontend applications, mobile clients, and B2B integrators, the Frugal Fortress adheres to a strict API versioning and deprecation policy.
## 1. URI Versioning
All REST endpoints are prefixed with the API version (e.g., `/api/v1/`).
*   **Current Version:** `v1`
*   We do not use header-based or query-based versioning, as URI versioning provides the most explicit caching and routing boundaries at the CDN/Edge layer.
## 2. Non-Breaking Changes (Allowed in current version)
The following changes will be made to `/api/v1/` without bumping the version:
*   Adding new endpoints.
*   Adding new optional fields to request payloads.
*   Adding new fields to response payloads.
*   Changing the order of fields in a JSON response.
*Clients must be designed to ignore unrecognized fields in JSON responses.*
## 3. Breaking Changes (Requires new version)
The following changes require bumping the API to `/api/v2/`:
*   Removing or renaming an endpoint.
*   Removing or renaming a field in a response payload.
*   Changing the data type of a field (e.g., from `integer` to `string`).
*   Adding a new *required* field to a request payload.
## 4. Deprecation Policy (Sunset)
When an endpoint or an entire API version is scheduled for removal:
1.  **Notice:** We will add the `Deprecation: true` HTTP header to the response.
2.  **Sunset Header:** We will include the `Sunset: <HTTP-date>` header indicating the exact date the endpoint will stop functioning.
3.  **Grace Period:** A deprecated API version will be supported for a minimum of **6 months** after the release of the new version to allow clients sufficient time to migrate.
```

### `docs/architecture/CORE_INVARIANTS.md`
```md
# Core Engineering Invariants
This document defines the unbreakable rules of the Frugal Fortress architecture. Any Pull Request violating these invariants will be rejected by the CI/CD pipeline or code review.
## 1. State & Resource Invariants
1. **Zero Leaked Sessions:** Every database session MUST be acquired via an `async with` context manager (`sessionmanager.session()`). No exceptions.
2. **Non-Blocking Event Loop:** CPU-bound operations (e.g., Token counting, ONNX Reranking, Cryptography) MUST be offloaded using `asyncio.to_thread()` or the `CpuBoundExecutor`. The FastAPI event loop must never block.
3. **Idempotent Side Effects:** Background jobs (`Arq`) that perform external side effects (LLM calls, DB writes) MUST be idempotent. Use `RedisIdempotencyGuard` keyed by the input hash (e.g., `SHA-256` of the audio/PDF bytes).
## 2. FinOps & Telemetry Invariants
1. **No Silent LLM Calls:** Every LLM request (success or failure) MUST be logged via the `FinOpsCallback`. It must record `model_name`, `prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens`, and `cost_usd`.
2. **Strict Tenant Isolation:** Every database query, feedback log, and cache key MUST include the `tenant_id`. Cross-tenant data leakage is mathematically impossible if repositories enforce `WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id`.
3. **Trace Context Propagation:** The `X-Request-ID` generated by the API Gateway MUST be propagated to all background workers and injected into every `structlog` entry and OpenTelemetry span.
## 3. Security Invariants
1. **Fail-Secure DLP:** If the local DLP Proxy (Llama-3.2-3B) crashes or times out, the system MUST throw a `SecurityPolicyViolation` (HTTP 403). It must never fail open and send unredacted text to external APIs.
2. **Application-Level Encryption (ALE):** Sensitive user data (Flashcards, Receipts, Glossary Rules) MUST be encrypted via `Fernet` (AES-128-CBC) before being passed to SQLAlchemy.
3. **No Secrets in Logs:** The `structlog_secret_redactor` MUST sanitize all log outputs. API keys and JWTs must never be written to `stdout` or Loki.
```

### `docs/architecture/SYSTEM_TOPOLOGY.md`
```md
# System Topology & Ecosystem
The "Frugal Fortress" ecosystem is designed to push compute to the Edge whenever possible, reserving the Cloud solely for secure, heavy orchestration, data persistence, and observability.
## 1. Repository Inventory
The core ecosystem is strictly divided into 2 repositories to enforce separation of concerns between the delivery mechanism and the business logic:
1.  📦 **`backend-monolith` (Python/FastAPI):** The secure core. Handles Audio Notes, Receipt Parsing, PDF-Anki, and GenUI orchestration. Deployed in a secure, containerized Virtual Private Cloud (VPC).
2.  💻 **`frontend-platform` (Next.js):** The interactive delivery layer. Contains the UIs for GenUI, Receipt Parser, and PDF-Anki. Deployed globally via Vercel's Edge Network.
*Note: Other isolated edge applications may ping the backend's telemetry endpoints (`/api/v1/log`) for centralized observability (Grafana/Loki), but they are not considered part of this core product ecosystem.*
## 2. Network & Deployment Topology
```text
[ END USER ]
    │
    └── Access via Web Browser / PWA ────────────────────┐
                                                         │
        [ Vercel Cloud ] (Serverless / Edge CDN)         │
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
        │ REPO 2: frontend-platform (Next.js)      │     │
        │  ├─ /genui                               │     │
        │  ├─ /pdf-anki                            │     │
        │  └─ /receipts (PWA)                      │     │
        └────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘     │
                             │ (HTTPS / JSON / SSE)      │
                             ▼                           │
        [ Secure VPC ] (Containerized Cluster)           │
        ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
        │ REPO 1: backend-monolith (Python)        │◄────┘
        │  ├─ FastAPI (API Gateway & Router)       │
        │  ├─ Audio Notes Domain                   │
        │  ├─ PDF to Anki Domain                   │
        │  ├─ Receipt Parser Domain                │
        │  ├─ GenUI Domain                         │
        │  └─ DLP Proxy (Security Guardrail)       │
        └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```
## 3. Edge vs. Cloud Compute Distribution
To maintain the $5/month infrastructure baseline, compute is strictly distributed:
*   **Edge Compute (Frontend):**
    *   Image resizing and compression (Canvas API) for receipts.
    *   Static UI rendering and caching.
    *   Client-side form validation.
*   **Cloud Compute (Backend):**
    *   PII Scrubbing (Local SLM / Presidio).
    *   Vector Search (pgvector / LanceDB).
    *   LLM Orchestration and Self-Correction Loops.
    *   Application-Level Encryption (ALE).
```

### `docs/architecture/WHITEPAPER.md`
```md
# Architecture Whitepaper: The Frugal Fortress
**Domain:** Enterprise AI, FinOps, Site Reliability Engineering (SRE)
**Architecture:** Modular Monolith (Strict Hexagonal / Ports & Adapters)
## 1. The Challenge
Building a heavy document processing system (e.g., PDFs to AI-generated study flashcards, OCR receipt parsing) that scales to thousands of concurrent users presents two major hurdles:
1.  **Economic Viability:** Naive LLM API usage destroys profit margins.
2.  **Compliance:** Processing sensitive data (Invoices, Medical PDFs) requires strict adherence to SOC2 and Zero Data Retention policies.
## 2. The Architecture: Modular Monolith over Microservices
We deliberately chose a **Modular Monolith** (Python/FastAPI) over premature microservices. This eliminates network latency between domains, simplifies deployments, and maximizes resource sharing (Database connection pools, Redis multiplexing).
We enforce a strict **Hexagonal Architecture**. The core domain (e.g., `process_pdf_use_case`) is completely agnostic to the underlying infrastructure. Whether the LLM provider is Groq, OpenAI, or Vertex AI, the business logic remains pure and untouched.
## 3. Resilience Patterns (SRE)
In AI, third-party providers *will* fail. A naive application collapses; a resilient system adapts.
*   **Distributed Circuit Breakers:** Implemented a Redis-backed circuit breaker. If the primary LLM provider experiences a rate limit (HTTP 429) or outage, the circuit opens, automatically triggering a "Graceful Degradation" fallback to a cheaper/faster secondary model. The user never sees a 500 error.
*   **Idempotent Background Workers:** Heavy parsing is offloaded to `Arq` workers. We implemented strict idempotency keys based on the `SHA-256` hash of the file bytes. If a worker crashes or a user double-clicks, the system guarantees the file is only processed—and billed—once.
## 4. Innovation: The "Nested Learning Loop"
Fine-tuning LLMs is slow and expensive. Instead, we built a self-correcting RAG system.
When a user edits an AI-generated flashcard or corrects a receipt to fix a formatting or factual error, the backend vectorizes this correction and stores it in `pgvector` as an approved 'Few-Shot' example. The next time that user uploads a document, the system dynamically injects their past corrections into the System Prompt. **The system learns the user's style in real-time, at zero training cost.**
## 5. Zero-Compromise Testing
To guarantee enterprise reliability, standard unit tests are not enough.
*   **Mutation Testing:** We integrated `mutmut` to actively modify the source code during CI/CD, ensuring our test suite actually catches regressions, preventing "vanity coverage" metrics.
*   **LLM-as-a-Judge:** We integrated `DeepEval` for semantic evaluation, ensuring the quality of the generated outputs does not degrade when we swap underlying LLM models.
```

### `docs/ReceiptParser/tiers.md`
```md
Estrategia de tiers
## Campos Stripe
receipt_parser_user_id
language_code
tier
## Free
Flujo:
- Imagen/PDF -> LlamaParse -> markdown -> tokenización PII -> Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` -> JSON -> cifrado -> Supabase.
Límites:
- 30 recibos al mes.
- 5 al día.
- Retención de 3 meses.
- Capacidad máxima almacenada: 100 recibos.
- Máximo 3 versiones por `image_hash`.
Disclaimer:
- el tier gratuito usa OCR/LLM de terceros.
- no debe usarse para documentos especialmente sensibles.
## Tiers de pago: solo Vertex
Premium:
- modelo incluido: `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite`
Pro:
- modelo incluido: `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash`
PAYG:
- solo modelos Vertex seleccionables:
  - `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite`
  - `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash`
  - `gemini/gemini-2.5-pro`
No se usa ningún modelo de Anthropic ni OpenAI en ningún tier de pago.
## Límites por tier
Premium:
- 500 recibos al mes.
- 50 al día.
- Retención de 1 año.
- Capacidad máxima almacenada: 2.000 recibos.
- Máximo 10 versiones por `image_hash`.
Pro:
- 2.000 recibos al mes.
- 200 al día.
- Retención de 5 años.
- Capacidad máxima almacenada: 5.000 recibos.
- Máximo 50 versiones por `image_hash`.
Ultra:
- 200 al día.
- capacidad máxima almacenada: 10.000 recibos.
- máximo 100 versiones por `image_hash`.
PAYG:
- 1.000 recibos al día.
- sin límite mensual fijo.
- la retención depende del tier base.
- máximo 40 versiones por `image_hash`.
## Versionado y correcciones
Cada persistencia en `receipts` cuenta como una fila:
- el parseo inicial.
- cada corrección humana guardada en `POST /receipts/history`.
El bucle interno de auto-corrección del modelo no crea filas nuevas.
## FinOps y precio mínimo sin pérdidas
Fuente oficial de pricing Vertex:
- https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/pricing
Precios usados (por millón de tokens):
- Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite: input `$0.10 / 1M`, output `$0.40 / 1M`.
- Gemini 2.5 Flash: input `$0.30 / 1M`, output `$2.50 / 1M`.
Supuesto conservador para un recibo de una sola imagen:
- 1 imagen de entrada equivalente a `1.120` tokens (proxy publicado para Gemini Flash Image).
- `500` tokens de salida JSON.
- hasta `3` intentos pagados por recibo (Self-Correction Loop con `MAX_RETRIES = 3`).
### Premium
Coste máximo por recibo:
- un intento: `(1.120 * 0.10 / 1.000.000) + (500 * 0.40 / 1.000.000) = $0.000312`
- peor caso con 3 intentos: `$0.000936`
Coste máximo mensual:
- `500 * $0.000936 = $0.468`
Precio mínimo recomendado sin pérdidas:
- **$0.99 / mes**
PRECIO FINAL:
- **$2.99 / mes**
Justificación:
- `$0.99` cubre el techo de inferencia incluso contando tres intentos y deja margen operativo.
- Flash-Lite es el modelo más barato del catálogo Vertex, lo que hace Premium el tier de mayor margen relativo.
### Pro
#### Modelo anterior vs modelo nuevo
El tier Pro cambió de `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5` a `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash`.
Este es el tier que más se ve afectado en el cambio de precio.
Coste máximo por recibo con Claude Haiku 4.5 (modelo anterior):
- Precios Claude Haiku 4.5: input `$1.00 / 1M`, output `$5.00 / 1M`.
- un intento: `(1.120 * 1.00 / 1.000.000) + (500 * 5.00 / 1.000.000) = $0.003620`
- peor caso con 3 intentos: `$0.010860`
- coste máximo mensual (2.000 recibos): `2.000 * $0.010860 = $21.72`
- **Precio mínimo anterior: ~$24.99 / mes**
Coste máximo por recibo con Gemini 2.5 Flash (modelo actual):
- un intento: `(1.120 * 0.30 / 1.000.000) + (500 * 2.50 / 1.000.000) = $0.001586`
- peor caso con 3 intentos: `$0.004758`
Coste máximo mensual:
- `2.000 * $0.004758 = $9.516`
Precio mínimo recomendado sin pérdidas:
- **$9.99 / mes**
Justificación:
- El cambio de Claude Haiku a Gemini 2.5 Flash reduce el coste por recibo de `$0.010860` a `$0.004758` (mejora del 56%).
- El coste máximo mensual cae de `$21.72` a `$9.516`, permitiendo bajar el precio de `$24.99` a `$9.99`.
- Aun así, el precio no puede bajar agresivamente porque el límite mensual es alto (2.000 recibos) y se permiten hasta 3 intentos por recibo.
- `$9.99` es el primer precio comercial simple que cubre el techo `$9.516` sin dejar el plan en pérdidas.
- Si se quisiera bajar Pro por debajo de `$9.99`, habría que reducir el límite mensual de recibos o el número máximo de intentos del flujo de corrección.
Conclusión sobre el cambio de modelo en Pro:
- Gemini 2.5 Flash tiene un coste por token mucho más bajo que Claude Haiku 4.5.
- El gran número de recibos permitidos en Pro (2.000/mes) y los 3 reintentos son los factores dominantes en el precio final.
- La reducción de precio de `$24.99` a `$9.99` es directamente atribuible al cambio de modelo.
## Data lifecycle
Tras cancelar:
- se mantiene la ventana de gracia de 90 días.
- durante ese periodo el usuario baja a límites de cómputo Free, pero conserva acceso y exportación de su histórico.
- al día 91 se aplica poda FIFO hasta el límite del tier Free.
```

### `docs/ReceiptParser/finops_capacity_planning.md`
```md
# docs/ReceiptParser/finops_capacity_planning.md
# FinOps & Capacity Planning: Receipt Parser
This document projects the infrastructure and LLM costs for the `receipt_parser` module.
## 1. Baseline Assumptions
*   **1 Receipt Image** ≈ 1,120 tokens (Standard VLM image encoding).
*   **JSON Output** ≈ 500 tokens.
*   **Target Volume:** 10,000 receipts processed per month.
*   **Self-Correction Loop:** Assume 10% of receipts require 1 retry due to math validation failures.
## 2. LLM Provider Cost Comparison (Per Receipt)
| Provider / Model | Tier | Input Cost (Image) | Output Cost (JSON) | Total Cost (per receipt) | Total Cost (10k receipts) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LlamaParse + Groq** (`llama-3.3`) | Free | $0.0000 (Free Tier) | $0.0004 | **$0.0004** | **$4.00** |
| **Vertex AI** (`gemini-2.5-flash-lite`) | Premium | $0.00011 | $0.00020 | **$0.00031** | **$3.10** |
| **Vertex AI** (`gemini-2.5-flash`) | Pro | $0.00033 | $0.00125 | **$0.00158** | **$15.80** |
## 3. Architectural Savings (The "Frugal" Impact)
1. **Edge Image Compression:** By using the HTML5 Canvas API on the Next.js PWA, we resize images to a max of 1024px before uploading. This reduces payload size from ~5MB to ~150KB, saving massive bandwidth costs and preventing VLM token explosion.
2. **Deterministic Math Validation:** Instead of asking the LLM to "check its math" (which consumes tokens and is unreliable), we use a pure Python validator (`validate_receipt_math`). We only spend retry tokens when a mathematical error is strictly proven.
```

### `docs/ReceiptParser/supabase_receipt_parser_encrypt_timestamps_payg.sql`
```sql
-- Encrypt payg, created_at, updated_at for receipt_parser_users.
--
-- These columns are now stored as Fernet ciphertext (app-layer encryption).
-- Existing rows are converted to plaintext strings here; the application's
-- decrypt_field / decrypt_bool helpers gracefully handle non-Fernet values
-- by returning the raw string, so no data is lost during the transition.
--
-- Run once in the Supabase SQL Editor (or via psql).
-- payg: BOOLEAN → TEXT
-- USING converts existing TRUE/FALSE to "true"/"false" strings.
-- NULL stays NULL. decrypt_bool("true") → True, decrypt_bool("false") → False.
ALTER TABLE public.receipt_parser_users
    ALTER COLUMN payg TYPE TEXT
    USING CASE
        WHEN payg IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN payg THEN 'true'
        ELSE 'false'
    END;
-- created_at: TIMESTAMPTZ → TEXT
-- Existing values become ISO-8601 strings (e.g. "2024-01-15 10:30:00+00").
-- decrypt_field returns them as-is (not Fernet tokens), which is the
-- expected legacy-fallback behaviour.
ALTER TABLE public.receipt_parser_users
    ALTER COLUMN created_at TYPE TEXT
    USING CASE
        WHEN created_at IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE created_at::text
    END;
-- updated_at: TIMESTAMPTZ → TEXT (same rationale as created_at)
ALTER TABLE public.receipt_parser_users
    ALTER COLUMN updated_at TYPE TEXT
    USING CASE
        WHEN updated_at IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE updated_at::text
    END;
```

### `docs/ReceiptParser/troubleshooting.md`
```md
# Troubleshooting: Receipt processing (POST /receipts/process)
## Error: `receipt_process_unexpected_error` with "Name or service not known"
**Symptom:** The API returns 200 with `status: "unprocessable"` and `error_message: "[STUB] Receipt processing failed due to unexpected error..."`. In `logs/api.json` you see:
```text
ConnectError: [Errno -2] Name or service not known
```
and in the traceback: `host = 'api.llamaindex.ai'`, `url = 'https://api.llamaindex.ai/v1/upload'`.
**Cause:** The host where the backend runs **cannot resolve the DNS name `api.llamaindex.ai`**. The API keys (e.g. `LLAMAPARSE_RECEIPT_PARSER_API_KEY`, `GROQ_RECEIPT_PARSER_API_KEY`) are fine; the failure is **network/DNS** before any HTTPS request completes.
### Fixes
1. **Run with Docker Compose (recommended)**
   The `api` service in `docker-compose.yml` is configured with DNS servers `8.8.8.8` and `8.8.4.4` so the container can resolve external hostnames. Restart the stack:
   ```bash
   docker compose down && docker compose up -d api
   ```
   Then call `POST /api/v1/receipts/process` again.
2. **Run uvicorn directly (e.g. on WSL or host)**
   Ensure the machine has outbound DNS and HTTPS (no firewall blocking `api.llamaindex.ai`). From the same machine, test:
   ```bash
   curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://api.llamaindex.ai/v1/health
   ```
   If this fails or times out, fix DNS/network on that machine (e.g. WSL: check `/etc/resolv.conf`; corporate: proxy or VPN).
3. **Optional: override LlamaParse API URL**
   If you must use a proxy or a different endpoint, set in `.env`:
   ```env
   LLAMAPARSE_BASE_URL=https://your-proxy.example.com/llamaparse/v1
   ```
   Then restart the backend. The receipt flow will use this base URL for upload and job status.
4. **Slow LlamaParse jobs / frontend timeout**
   Receipt OCR can legitimately take more than 100 seconds before Groq receives
   markdown. The backend exposes receipt-specific LlamaParse timeouts:
   ```env
   RECEIPT_PARSER_LLAMAPARSE_REQUEST_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=180
   RECEIPT_PARSER_LLAMAPARSE_POLL_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=300
   ```
   Keep the Next.js `/api/v1/receipts/process` proxy and client deadline above
   the expected backend window, otherwise the browser can show a timeout while
   FastAPI continues processing the same receipt.
### Flow summary
- **Free tier:** Image → LlamaParse (LlamaIndex Cloud) → markdown → Groq (LLM) → JSON → math validation → response.
- **Keys used:** `LLAMAPARSE_RECEIPT_PARSER_API_KEY` or `LLAMAPARSE_API_KEY` for LlamaParse; Groq is used via the app’s existing LiteLLM/GROQ configuration for the text step.
After DNS/network is fixed, the same request (with valid `image_base64`) should return either `status: "verified"` with a `receipt` or `status: "unprocessable"` with a clear validation/LLM error message (no more ConnectError).
```

ALTER TABLE public.receipt_parser_users

ALTER TABLE public.receipt_parser_users

### `docs/ReceiptParser/README.md`
```md
# Module: Receipt Parser (Cost-Efficient Financial Extraction)
## 1. Architectural Overview
The Receipt Parser is a hybrid AI architecture designed to extract structured financial data with maximum privacy and minimum cost. It leverages Edge computing to reduce payload sizes and Cloud orchestration for intelligent extraction.
**Core Flow:**
1. **Edge Compute:** The Next.js PWA captures the image, resizes it via the HTML5 Canvas API (max 1024px), and exports a highly compressed JPEG (~150KB), slashing bandwidth and VLM token consumption.
2. **Gateway:** FastAPI receives the Base64 payload and routes it based on the user's tier.
3. **Adaptive Routing:**
   * *Free Tier:* LlamaParse -> Markdown -> PII Scrubbing (Presidio) -> Groq (Llama-3.3) -> JSON.
   * *Paid Tiers:* Direct to Vertex AI (Gemini 2.5 Flash/Pro) Vision-Language Model -> JSON.
4. **Deterministic Validation:** A pure Python `MathValidator` ensures `Subtotal + Tax + Shipping = Total`.
## 2. The Self-Correction Loop & HITL
LLMs are prone to mathematical hallucinations. To counter this:
* **Auto-Retry:** If the `MathValidator` fails, the system appends the specific error to the prompt and retries the LLM up to 3 times.
* **Human-in-the-Loop (HITL):** If validation still fails, the API does not return a 500 error. It returns an **HTTP 206 Partial Content** with the AI's "best effort" JSON. The frontend renders an editable form, the user corrects the math, and submits it to `/receipts/history`.
## 3. Few-Shot Vendor Matching (pgvector)
When a receipt is successfully saved, the `issuer_name` is embedded using `FastEmbed` (1536D) and stored in PostgreSQL via `pgvector`. On subsequent uploads, the system performs a cosine similarity search to find previously validated receipts from the same vendor. These are injected into the LLM prompt as `<example>` blocks, allowing the system to dynamically adapt to obscure receipt layouts without fine-tuning.
```

### `docs/ReceiptParser/DEMO docs/DEMO.md`
```md
# UAT Sign-off & Technical Showcase: Receipt Parser Module
## 🛠️ 1. Complementary Software (The "Show, Don't Tell" Stack)
During the Live Demo, keep tabs open with the following tools:
*   **Grafana + Prometheus + Loki (Infrastructure Observability):**
    *   *What to show:* A dashboard tracking the `Self-Correction Loop` retries and the HTTP 206 (Partial Content) response rates.
    *   *The Pitch:* "We don't trust LLMs blindly. Here we monitor our deterministic Math Validator. If the LLM hallucinates a tax calculation, we catch it, force a retry, and track the failure rate."
*   **Langfuse / Arize Phoenix (AI Observability & FinOps):**
    *   *What to show:*
- El Bucle de Auto-Corrección (Self-Correction Loop): Debes forzar un fallo matemático (ej. enviando un recibo donde Subtotal + Tax != Total). La traza en Phoenix debe mostrar múltiples llamadas al LLM (reintentos), donde el prompt de la segunda llamada incluye el error inyectado por el validador Python: [Previous error: Subtotal - discount + tax does not match total... Fix the totals.].
- Few-Shot Vendor Matching (RAG): La traza debe mostrar cómo se inyectan recibos anteriores del mismo proveedor (ej. "Uber") dentro de las etiquetas <example> en el prompt del sistema, recuperados vía pgvector.
## 📄 2. Leave-Behind Artifacts (Enterprise Grade)
*   **C4 Model Diagrams:** Showing the Edge (PWA) -> Gateway -> Math Validator -> LLM Provider flow.
*   **ADRs (Architecture Decision Records):**
    *   *Example:* "Why we use Edge Image Compression (Canvas API) before uploading?" (Answer: Reduces payload size from 5MB to 150KB, slashing bandwidth costs and VLM token consumption).
*   **Security & Compliance Sheet:** Detailing the PII Scrubbing process (Presidio) for free-tier users before sending OCR text to Groq.
## 📜 3. Detailed Script & Diagram Mapping (The "Staff Engineer" Pitch)
**🎬 Act 1: The Hook & Hexagonal Architecture (0:00 - 2:00)**
*   **Visual:** Open `docs/diagrams/Receipt_Parser/diagram_hexagonal_module.md`.
*   **The Pitch:** "Expense processing is traditionally error-prone. Today, I'll show you a hybrid AI architecture. As you can see in our Hexagonal Module diagram, the core domain (the Math Validator and Self-Correction Loop) is completely isolated from the inbound FastAPI router and the outbound LLM providers. This allows us to route Free users to LlamaParse and Paid users to Vertex AI seamlessly."
**🎬 Act 2: Edge Compute & The "Happy Path" (2:00 - 4:00)**
*   **Visual:** Next.js PWA interface (Mobile view in browser dev tools).
*   **Action:** Upload a receipt image.
*   **The Pitch:** "Notice the speed. Before the image even hits our server, the browser's Canvas API resizes and compresses it. We send a 150KB Base64 payload instead of a 5MB photo, saving massive bandwidth and VLM tokens."
**🎬 Act 3: The Self-Correction Loop & HTTP 206 (4:00 - 7:00)**
*   **Visual:** Open `docs/diagrams/Receipt_Parser/diagram_response_contract.md` and the PWA UI.
*   **Action:** Enable the `MOCK_RECEIPTS_PROCESS_206` flag to simulate an LLM math hallucination.
*   **The Pitch:** "LLMs are bad at math. If the AI hallucinates a tax calculation, our deterministic Python Math Validator catches it and forces a retry.
    If it fails 3 times, look at our Response Contract diagram: we don't throw a 500 error. We return an HTTP 206 Partial Content. The frontend renders a Human-in-the-Loop form with the AI's 'best effort' data. The user corrects the math, clicks save, and the system learns."
**🎬 Act 4: History, Decryption & Export (7:00 - 9:00)**
*   **Visual:** Open `docs/diagrams/Receipt_Parser/diagram_history_flow.md` and `diagram_export_csv.md`.
*   **The Pitch:** "For Fortune 500 clients, privacy is key. When a user views their history or exports a CSV, the data is decrypted on the fly using Application-Level Encryption (ALE), as shown in our History Flow diagram. Even a DBA cannot read the receipt totals in the database."
## 🧩 4. GenUI Integration (The Meta-Portfolio)
When a recruiter asks about "Financial Data Extraction" in your GenUI portfolio:
*   **`ReceiptHitlForm` (Static):** Render the actual Human-in-the-Loop React component, pre-filled with a math error, allowing the recruiter to "fix" the math themselves in the proposal.
*   **`EdgeComputeSavingsChart` (Dynamic):** The LLM generates a chart showing bandwidth and VLM token savings achieved by your Edge Canvas compression strategy vs sending raw 4K images.
```

### `docs/ReceiptParser/DEMO docs/script_architecture_deep_dive.md`
```md
# The Staff Engineer Pitch: Receipt Parser (Live Demo Script)
*Target Audience: CTOs, VPs of Engineering, Enterprise Architects.*
*Tone: Authoritative, FinOps-focused, Security-conscious.*
**Tasks:**
- Enable `DEBUG=false` in `.env` to ensure JSON structured logging.
- Have a Free user and a PAYG user ready.
---
## Act 1: The Hook & Architectural Vision (0:00 - 2:00)
**(Visual: Open `docs/architecture/WHITEPAPER.md` briefly, then switch to `docs/diagrams/modules/Receipt_Parser/c4_model.md` in the IDE. Scroll from the Container Diagram down to the Component Diagram. Finally, show `docs/architecture/HEXAGONAL_DECOUPLING.md`)**
"Financial data extraction is traditionally brittle and expensive. Today, I'll introduce you to Vera (Verified Expense & Receipt Architecture), the Receipt Parser module of our 'Frugal Fortress'.
As outlined in our **Architecture Whitepaper**, instead of building a latency-heavy microservices web, we engineered a highly optimized **Modular Monolith**. Vera operates as a strictly isolated bounded context within this system.
If we look at the **C4 Component Diagram** for this module, you will see our strict adherence to **Hexagonal Architecture**. As detailed in our **Hexagonal Decoupling** documentation, the core domain—our deterministic Math Validator and the Self-Correction Loop—is completely isolated from the inbound FastAPI router and the outbound LLM providers.
This decoupling is the cornerstone of our FinOps and Security strategy. It allows the Application layer to dynamically route Free-tier users to a LlamaParse and Groq stack, while routing paid users directly to Google Vertex AI for Zero Data Retention, all without altering a single line of core business logic."
## Act 2: Edge Compute & Offline PWA Recovery (2:00 - 3:30)
**(Action: In Browser DevTools -> Network tab, change throttling to 'Offline'. Upload the heavy 7MB receipt photo. Point out that the UI queues it without throwing network errors.)**
**(Visual: Open `docs/diagrams/modules/Receipt_Parser/diagram_frontend_offline_sync.md`)**
"In the real world, networks fail. If an employee is in the subway without coverage, our PWA doesn't crash. As shown in our **Frontend Offline Sync diagram**, it retains the payload at the Edge using IndexedDB.
**(Action: Change DevTools Network back to 'No throttling'. Watch the POST request fire instantly. Switch to Grafana/Loki and run `{job="app"} |= "/api/v1/receipts/process" |= "Audit Log"` to show the backend receiving it.)**
The millisecond we regain connection, our offline sync hook drains the queue and the request fires to the backend, as we can see here in our Loki audit logs. Notice the `[TRUNCATED]` tag in the log body; our Audit Middleware automatically truncates massive payloads to protect our observability stack from DoS attacks.
**(Visual: Open `docs/architecture/SYSTEM_TOPOLOGY.md` to show the Edge vs Cloud split, then flash `docs/ReceiptParser/DEMO docs/finops_capacity_planning.md`)**
But more importantly, look at our Edge Compute strategy. As mapped out in our System Topology, we push compute to the Edge whenever possible.
**(Action: Switch back to Browser DevTools -> Network. Point to the 7.02 MB PNG data URL, then the 236 KB JPEG data URL, and finally click the `POST process` request and highlight the `Content-Length: 236928` header.)**
I just uploaded a 7-Megabyte PNG. But before it even hit the network, the browser's Canvas API resized and compressed it locally. As you can see in the Network tab, the resulting JPEG and the actual HTTP Content-Length is only 236 Kilobytes.
This slashes bandwidth costs and, as projected in our **FinOps Capacity Planning** sheet, reduces Vision-Language Model token consumption by over 90%."
## Act 2.5: State Recovery & Client-Side Math (3:30 - 4:30)
**(Action: While in the HTTP 206 correction form, refresh the browser page. The form state remains intact.)**
**(Visual: Show src/app/receipts/receipt-parse-memory.ts in the IDE, highlighting the ReceiptParseMemory type)**
"What happens if the user accidentally refreshes the page while correcting a receipt? Nothing is lost. Our receipt-parse-memory module continuously snapshots the entire React Hook Form state to LocalStorage. The UI rehydrates instantly without a single database query."
**(Visual: Switch to src/app/receipts/receipt-calc.ts in the IDE, highlighting parseDecimalStringToCents and deriveReceiptMath)**
"Furthermore, to avoid hammering our backend with validation requests, we ported our deterministic math logic to the frontend in receipt-calc.ts.
Because JavaScript is notorious for floating-point errors, we convert all currencies to cents using native BigInt precision.
**(Visual: Switch to show the form and edit a line, then click Recalculate)**
When the user edits a line item, they can explicitly click 'Recalculate'.
The frontend then computes the exact taxes and subtotals using deterministic rounding. This ensures the payload is mathematically sound before it ever crosses the network, saving bandwidth and backend compute cycles."
Q&A
-------
- Why is the backend math validator in pure Python instead of Rust?
As documented in ADR-0008: Native Acceleration Strategy, we profiled the execution. Pydantic and Python's native Decimal library are fast enough for financial math. We reserve native acceleration (like ONNX) strictly for vector embeddings.
## Act 3: PII Scrubbing & Threat Modeling (The Enterprise Trade-off) (4:30 - 5:30)
**(Action: Upload a receipt 'billing.png' containing a person name,email/phone number using a Free user.)**
**(Visual: Open `docs/security/AI_THREAT_MODELING.md` to the 'Sensitive Information Disclosure' section, then switch to Arize Phoenix `http://localhost:6006` to show the Groq trace)**
"For Fortune 500 clients, privacy is the ultimate blocker. Let's look at our Threat Model.
On our Free Tier, we use third-party APIs like LlamaParse for OCR. We are completely transparent that this involves sending the raw image to an external service. However, before that extracted text ever reaches our reasoning LLM—Groq—it passes through our internal PII Scrubber.
If we look at the Input Prompt in Arize Phoenix, you won't see the person's real email or phone number. You see `<PERSON_2>`, `<EMAIL_1>` and `<PHONE_3>`. We use Microsoft Presidio and deterministic regex to tokenize this data locally. We protect the user's identity from the LLM provider, even on the free tier.
But here is the architectural flex: For strict SOC2 or HIPAA compliance where the *image itself* cannot touch a standard third-party API, our application layer dynamically routes Paid users directly to Google Vertex AI. Vertex operates under a strict Enterprise BAA with Zero Data Retention. We offer FinOps flexibility for free users, and absolute cryptographic sovereignty for Enterprise clients, all from the same codebase."
## Act 4: The Self-Correction Loop & HTTP 206 (5:30 - 7:30)
**(Action: Update the user to PREMIUM and Upload a receipt 'e-receipt shell' )**
**(Visual: Show Arize Phoenix (multiple retries) and the Next.js UI showing the HTTP 206 form. Open `docs/diagrams/modules/Receipt_Parser/diagram_response_contract.md`)**
"LLMs hallucinate math. As noted in our Threat Model under 'Insecure Output Handling', we never trust the AI's calculations. If the AI hallucinates a tax calculation, our deterministic Python Math Validator catches it.
In Phoenix, you can see the trace has more than one child spans. Look at the prompt for the second call: we injected the Python error *'[Previous JSON did not match the required schema. Error: 1 validation error for ReceiptSchema]'* back into the LLM.
If it fails a maximum of 3 times, look at our **Response Contract diagram**: we don't throw a 500 error. We return an HTTP 206 Partial Content. The frontend renders a Human-in-the-Loop form with the AI's 'best effort' data. The user applies the correction, clicks save, and the system learns."
## Act 5: Deterministic Memory & Blind Indexing (7:30 - 9:00)
**(Action: Correct the last receipt: a receipt's Vendor to "Shell GOAT", Buyer to "Javier Martin", and add a CIF. Save it. Then, upload a new receipt 'e-receipt shell -big')**
**(Visual: Open Arize Phoenix and show the trace for the new receipt. Click on the `vertex_ai/gemini-2.5-flash-lite` span. Go to the 'Output' tab and highlight the JSON where it says `"issuer_name": "Shell"`, `"nif_cif_ssn": "null"` and `"payer_name": "N/A"`.)**
"How does the system learn from user corrections? You might expect us to just inject past receipts into the LLM prompt using RAG. But injecting examples costs input tokens, and LLMs can still ignore them or hallucinate. For critical text fields, we took a more frugal and deterministic approach.
Look at this trace in Arize Phoenix for the receipt I just uploaded. We are using our Premium tier, so the image was sent directly to Google's Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite. If you look at the LLM's raw output, it extracted `"issuer_name": "Shell"`, `"nif_cif_ssn": "null"` and `"payer_name": "N/A"`.
**(Visual: Switch to the Next.js UI showing the perfectly extracted "Shell GOAT", CIF number inserted and "Javier Martin".)**
But if we look at our UI, it perfectly displays my custom vendor name, 'Shell GOAT', and my name as the buyer. How did it know?
**(Visual: Switch to the `feedback_log` table in Supabase. Highlight the `user_prompt` column showing `gAAAAAB...` and then highlight the `user_prompt_fingerprint` column showing the long hex string.)**
The gap between the AI's mistake and the perfect UI is our Deterministic Memory engine. But there is a massive enterprise security challenge here.
If you look at our database, the actual corrections are encrypted at rest using Application-Level Encryption. You just see Fernet tokens starting with `gAAAAAB`. Because this encryption is randomized, we cannot run a standard SQL `WHERE` clause to find past corrections.
To solve this, we implemented a cryptographic pattern called **Blind Indexing**.
When I previously corrected 'Shell' to 'Shell GOAT', the backend generated a deterministic HMAC-SHA256 hash of the word 'Shell' and saved it in this `fingerprint` column.
Now, when the LLM extracts the standard name 'Shell', our backend hashes it, queries this fingerprint column, retrieves the encrypted correction, and decrypts 'Shell GOAT' strictly in memory before it reaches the frontend.
We achieve fine-tuning levels of personalization with O(1) database lookups, absolute data privacy, and it costs us exactly zero additional AI tokens."
## Act 6: FinOps Fallback & ALE Encryption (9:00 - 10:30)
**(Action: Set a PAYG user's balance to $0.00001. Request gemini-2.5-pro in the UI. Then click export to CSV.)**
**(Visual: Briefly flash docs/architecture/BILLING_AND_TIERS.md before showing the Loki logs with the query {job="app"} |= "receipt_payg_balance_insufficient_fallback".)**
"To prevent Denial of Wallet attacks and enforce the margins defined in our Billing & Tiers Strategy, our BudgetManager intercepts expensive requests if funds are insufficient. As detailed in ADR-0012, we use an atomic Two-Phase Commit in Redis to reserve funds before inference.
Here in Loki, you can see the `receipt_payg_balance_insufficient_fallback` event. The system saw the user had no balance, blocked the Gemini model, and gracefully degraded to the base tier model. We saved the user's experience and protected our profit margin simultaneously."
**(Visual: Switch to Supabase SQL Editor. Run the query SELECT id, user_role, subtotal, tax, total, payload_encrypted FROM receipts ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 3;. Highlight the gAAAAAB... tokens in the columns, and then highlight the Z0FBQUFB... in the payload.)**
"Finally, let's talk about data sovereignty. Look at the production database. The financial totals and the receipt payloads are encrypted with Application-Level Encryption (ALE) using AES-128-CBC Fernet tokens. Even a Database Administrator with full access cannot read the financial totals of our clients.
If you look closely, individual columns like subtotal and tax start with gAA, the standard Fernet signature. But look at the payload_encrypted column—it starts with Z0.
This is intentional. Because the full receipt payload is a massive, complex JSON structure, we wrap the Fernet token in an additional layer of strict Base64 encoding before persisting it to PostgreSQL. This guarantees binary-safe storage and prevents any character encoding corruption at the database driver level."
**(Visual: Open docs/diagrams/modules/Receipt_Parser/diagram_history_flow.md in the IDE. Point to the _decrypt_payload(ALE) node. Then open the downloaded CSV file to show the clear-text data.)**
"But when the user requests a CSV export, how do they get their data? As shown in our History Flow diagram, the payload is fetched from Postgres, the Base64 wrapper is stripped, and the Fernet token is decrypted on the fly, strictly in the application's memory.
If we open the file I just exported, the data is perfectly formatted in clear text. We guarantee absolute Zero-Trust security at rest, without sacrificing usability for the end user."
## Act 7: Security, GDPR & Data Lifecycle (10:30 - 11:30)
**(Visual: Open docs/architecture/MULTI_TENANCY_AND_ISOLATION.md and src/app/middleware/context.py in the IDE to show the ContextMiddleware.)**
"For Fortune 500 clients, data isolation and GDPR compliance are non-negotiable.
First, we use Python `contextvars` via our `ContextMiddleware` to bind the tenant_id to every request and background job. As outlined in our `Multi-Tenancy Strategy`, cross-tenant data leakage is mathematically impossible at the query level.
Second, GDPR compliance is fully automated. Let me show you."
**(Action: Open your SQL Client. Run "Replicate Receipts for Tenanted Records" script (para añadir 150 recibos al usuario), pasandole el tenant_id del user y un id de recibo para ser duplicado.**
**- Muestra el user Premium whose subscription_status is 'canceled' and current_period_end='2026-01-15T10:00:00+00:00'. Ejecuta el sql command 'Count Receipts for canceled-user decks', confirma que tiene 151 tarjetas (tambien en el frontend).**
"So, through an SQL command, we have just added 150 receipts to this user. We also set their subscription status to 'canceled' and backdated their billing period end date to more than 90 days ago. Let's see what happens when our background worker wakes up."
**Then, switch to the terminal and execute 'uv run python scripts/vera_force_prune.py' (or restart the worker).)**
**(Visual: Switch to Grafana/Loki and run the query {job="app"} |= "receipt_parser_prune_completed". Then, refresh the SQL Client to show that the count dropped from 150 to exactly 100 (the Free tier limit). Finally, switch to the Next.js UI and show the /usage banner reflecting the new count.)**
"In our Data Lifecycle module, an Arq cron job runs daily. If a user cancels their subscription, we give them a 90-day grace period.
As you just saw in the database and our Loki logs, on day 91, the system automatically applies a massive FIFO truncation. It permanently deleted their oldest receipts down to the 100-receipt limit of the Free tier to comply with data minimization laws.
There is no manual Database Administrator intervention required; privacy and compliance are built directly into the architecture."
## Act 8: Advanced QA Maturity & The Call to Action (11:30 - 13:00)
**(Visual: Switch from the IDE to your browser. Show the GitHub Actions UI with a green pipeline, specifically highlighting the "Mutation Testing (Core Logic)" job. Open `docs/operations/CI_CD_DEPLOYMENT_STRATEGY.md`. Then briefly flash the `reports/coverage_html/index.html` showing your ~90% coverage.)**
"Finally, how do we guarantee this architecture doesn't break when new engineers join the team? We prove our reliability through rigorous QA, orchestrated by our **Zero-Compromise CI/CD Pipeline**.
As you can see in our coverage report, we maintain a strict +90% baseline. But standard code coverage is a vanity metric; it tells you a line was executed, not that it was tested correctly.
That's why, in our CI/CD pipeline, we use Mutation Testing (`mutmut`) to actively modify the Abstract Syntax Tree during the build process. We don't waste CI compute mutating external API wrappers; We focus this exclusively on our Core domain.
**(Visual: Open `docs/testing/MUTATION_PRAGMAS.md` and `ADR-0005` in the IDE)**
As explained in our **Mutation Pragmas** doc and **ADR-0005** regarding Async Test Scoping, we mathematically prove that our deterministic Math Validator, our Self-Correction Loop retries, and our FinOps Lua scripts are unbreakable. If a test doesn't catch a mutation in the core, the build fails and the deployment is halted.
This is Vera: secure, auditable, and economically predictable. I would be happy to dive deeper into the codebase, our infrastructure setup, or discuss how these patterns can scale your current operations during a technical review. Thank you."
1. En `src/app/infrastructure/observability.py`, DEScomenta las líneas `hide_inputs`, `hide_outputs`, `hide_input_messages`, `hide_output_messages`, `hide_input_text`, `hide_output_text`.
```

```md
# docs/ReceiptParser/DEMO docs/script_elevator_pitch.md
# The Elevator Pitch: Receipt Parser (Video 1 Script)
*Target Audience: Recruiters, Hiring Managers, Non-Technical Stakeholders.*
*Tone: High-energy, problem-solving, business-value focused.*
*Duration: ~2.5 Minutes.*
1. En `src/app/infrastructure/observability.py`, comenta las líneas `hide_inputs`, `hide_outputs`, `hide_input_messages`, `hide_output_messages`, `hide_input_text`, `hide_output_text`.
---
## Act 1: The Hook & The Problem (0:00 - 0:45)
**(Visual: Start with your face on camera, then transition to a clean screen showing the Next.js PWA on a mobile emulator on the left, and a simple dashboard/Grafana on the right)**
"Expense processing is traditionally error-prone and expensive. If you send raw 4K photos of receipts to a Vision-Language Model, you are burning bandwidth and destroying your AI budget on millions of useless image tokens.
I'm Javier, and today I will introduce Vera(Verified Expense & Receipt Architecture): the receipt parser module of the 'Frugal Fortress'. It's a hybrid AI architecture that extracts structured financial data with maximum privacy and minimum cost. Let's see it."
## Act 2: The "Wow" Factor - Edge Compute FinOps (0:45 - 1:30)
**(Action: In the Next.js PWA, upload a 5MB photo of a receipt.)**
**(Visual: pestaña Network (Red) de las DevTools del navegador, el POST /api/v1/receipts/process - Request Headers y señalas Content-Lenght- 100-150Kb)**
"Notice the speed. Before the image even hits our server, the browser's Canvas API resizes and compresses it at the Edge.
Look at the Network tab. We sent a 150-kilobyte Base64 payload instead of a 5-megabyte photo. This slashes bandwidth costs and reduces Vision-Language Model token consumption by 80%. We pushed the compute to the client's device for free."
## Act 3: Deterministic Validation & Self-Correction (1:30 - 2:15)
**(PRE-RECORDING SETUP:**
2. Reinicia el backend: `docker compose restart api worker` en PowerShell.
3. Sube una factura que fuerza reintentos al LLM: billing.png
4. Ten lista la traza en Arize Phoenix POST /api/v1/receipts/process.
**)**
**(Action: Upload the receipt. The UI shows the Human-in-the-Loop form.)**
**((Visual: Point to the <PERSON_1>, <EMAIL_1>, and <PHONE_1> tags in the prompt.) )**
"LLMs are notoriously bad at strict formatting and math. We don't trust them blindly.
Look at this trace in our AI observability platform. First, notice the prompt is completely anonymized by Microsoft Presidio. You don't see James Martinez, his email, phone number or SSN; you see tags like <PERSON_1>, <EMAIL_1>, and <PHONE_1>. This is irrefutable proof for a SOC2 audit that zero PII leaked to the external AI provider.
VIDEO CUT
Sube la factura e-receipt shell -big.png.
Click the second `acompletion` span (the retry). Go to the "Attributes" tab -> expand `input.value`.)
**(Then scroll down to the bottom of the input messages to show the injected error: `[Previous JSON did not match the required schema...]`)**
I have uploaded another billing file. see there is more than one LLM call? When the AI hallucinated a data type or a tax calculation, our deterministic Python validator caught it and injected the exact error back into the prompt to force a correction.
If it fails 3 times, we don't throw a 500 server error. We return an HTTP 206 Partial Content.
(show frontend, formulario editable, editar Buyer por "Javier Martinez" o el Vendor por "Shell" si no se capturó)
The frontend renders a Human-in-the-Loop form with the AI's 'best effort' data. At this point the user may correct the math, click save, and the system learns this specific buyer's layout for next time using pgvector."
(VIDEO CUT)
Borra la factura guardada. Vuelve a subirla, espera al formulario editable y muestra que ahora el Buyer se ha capturado y su valor es "Javier Martinez (DEMO)".
After deleting this file we process it again but this time the system will remember our feedback and will display the Vendor's name automatically.
**(POST-RECORDING CLEANUP:**
1. DESCOMENTAR las líneas en `observability.py` para restaurar la seguridad SOC2.
2. Reiniciar de nuevo: `docker compose restart api worker`.
**)**
## Act 4: The Call to Action (2:15 - 2:30)
**(Visual: Back to your face on camera, with logos of FastAPI, Presidio, Postgres, GitHub Actions, and SOC2 floating next to you)**
"This system doesn't just work; it scales securely. It features PII Scrubbing via Microsoft Presidio to guarantee SOC2 compliance, Application-Level Encryption so even DBAs can't read your financial data, and a CI/CD pipeline with Mutation Testing to guarantee mathematical accuracy.
If you want to see the Hexagonal Architecture, the RAG Few-Shot matching, and the FinOps guardrails behind this, check out the 10-minute Architecture Deep Dive video below. Thanks for watching."

### `docs/ReceiptParser/DEMO docs/observabilidad.md`
```md
Escenario 3.1: PII Scrubbing (Fail-Secure)
    Acción: Sube una imagen de un recibo usando un usuario free. Factura.jpg es buen ejemplo.
    Dónde mirar: Arize Phoenix.
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        Abre la traza de la llamada a Groq (Llama-3.3).
        Revisa el Input (User Prompt). El texto OCR enviado al LLM NO debe contener el texto real de estos campos sensibles. Debe haber sido reemplazado por Microsoft Presidio y mostrarse como <EMAIL_1> y <PHONE_1>, etc.
        Esto es la prueba irrefutable para una auditoría SOC2 de que los datos sensibles no salen de tu VPC en el Tier gratuito.
Escenario 3.2: Bucle de Auto-Corrección (Math Validator) y Few-Shot Vendor Matching (RAG)
    Acción: Modifica el proveedor de la primera factura e inserta/modifica el formato del CIF y guarda los cambios.
    Acción: Subir recibo invalidado para modificarlo.
    Dónde mirar: Arize Phoenix y Grafana (Loki).
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        En Arize Phoenix: Verás una traza principal con múltiples spans (llamadas) hijas. Revisa el prompt de la segunda llamada; debe incluir el texto inyectado por tu código Python: [Previous error: Subtotal - discount + tax does not match total... Fix the totals.].
        En Grafana (Loki): Si falla 3 veces, busca el log que devuelve el HTTP 206 Partial Content ("status": "requires_human_correction").
    Dónde mirar: Arize Phoenix.
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        Abre la traza del segundo recibo.
        Revisa el System Prompt. Debes ver un bloque <example> que contiene el JSON del primer recibo que corregiste. Esto demuestra que pgvector encontró la similitud del issuer_name y adaptó el modelo al formato de ese proveedor específico.
Escenario 3.3: Graceful Degradation (FinOps Fallback)
    Acción: Configura un usuario con Tier payg y pon su balance_usd a $0.00 en la base de datos. Intenta procesar un recibo seleccionando el modelo gemini-2.5-pro.
    Dónde mirar: Grafana (Loki) y Arize Phoenix.
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        En Grafana (Loki): Busca el log "event": "receipt_payg_balance_insufficient_fallback". Esto demuestra la decisión de negocio: el BudgetManager bloqueó el uso del modelo caro por falta de fondos.
        En Arize Phoenix: Verifica que la traza resultante se ejecutó utilizando el modelo de fallback (ej. groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile) y el coste de la operación, salvando la experiencia del usuario.
Escenario 3.4: Edge Compression FinOps
    Acción: Sube una foto de 5MB desde la PWA (Frontend).
    Dónde mirar: Grafana (Loki) (Logs de la API) y Arize Phoenix.
    El Flex: En Loki, muestra el tamaño del payload recibido. En Phoenix, muestra los tokens de entrada. Explica: "El navegador comprimió la imagen vía Canvas API. Recibimos 150KB en lugar de 5MB. Esto reduce el coste del Vision-Language Model en un 80% y elimina la latencia de red."
 Escenario 3.5: Offline PWA Recovery (Resiliencia Extremo a Extremo)
    El Caso de Uso: Un empleado está en un avión o en el metro sin cobertura. Escanea un recibo de un café. La aplicación no debe fallar ni perder el dato.
    Acción:
        Abre las DevTools del navegador (F12) -> Pestaña Network -> Cambia a Offline.
        Sube 1 recibo en la interfaz. (La UI debe mostrar que está encolado/pendiente).
        Cambia la red de nuevo a Online (o "No throttling").
    Dónde mirar: Grafana (Loki) (Logs de la API) y la UI del Navegador.
    El Flex (El Pitch):
    "En el mundo real, las redes fallan. Aquí vemos cómo nuestra PWA retiene el payload comprimido en el Edge (IndexedDB/Service Worker). En el milisegundo en que recuperamos la conexión, la petición se dispara.
    Si miramos los logs en Loki, vemos cómo el backend recibe el payload. Y lo más importante a nivel de arquitectura: aunque miles de comerciales recuperaran la conexión al mismo tiempo al salir del metro, nuestro CpuBoundExecutor encola las validaciones matemáticas pesadas en procesos separados, garantizando que el Event Loop de FastAPI jamás se bloquee y la API siga respondiendo 200 OK al resto del sistema."
```
