### `docs/pdf-anki/tiers.md`
```md
Tiers
## Stripe Customized Fields on Payment Links
pdf_anki_user_id
language_code
tier
## Modelos por tier
Free:
- Flujo legacy: LlamaParse -> chunking -> LanceDB -> Groq `llama-3.3-70b-versatile`.
Admin:
- Mismos límites operativos que Pro.
- Misma infraestructura que Free.
Premium:
- Modelo incluido: `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite` vía Vertex AI.
Pro:
- Modelo incluido: `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash` vía Vertex AI.
PAYG:
- Solo modelos Vertex seleccionables:
  - `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite`
  - `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash`
  - `gemini/gemini-2.5-pro`
## Límites operativos
Free:
- 10 páginas por documento.
- 50 páginas al mes.
- 100 flashcards.
Premium:
- 100 páginas por PDF.
- 1.000 páginas al mes.
- 5.000 flashcards.
Pro:
- 500 páginas por PDF.
- 2.000 páginas al mes.
- 20.000 flashcards.
PAYG:
- 2.000 paginas al día (fair-use)
- 50.000 paginas al mes (fair-use)
- 50.000 flashcards de almacenamiento.
## FinOps y precio mínimo sin pérdidas
Fuente oficial de pricing Vertex:
- https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/generative-ai/pricing
Precios usados (por millón de tokens):
- Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite: input `$0.10 / 1M`, output `$0.40 / 1M`.
- Gemini 2.5 Flash: input `$0.30 / 1M`, output `$2.50 / 1M`.
Para el cálculo uso el mismo techo conservador que ya aplica el backend en `estimate_unified_pdf_token_budget()`:
- input máximo por petición: `602.000` tokens.
- output Premium: `500 + 100 * 250 = 25.500` tokens.
- output Pro: `500 + 300 * 250 = 75.500` tokens.
### Premium
Coste máximo por PDF:
- input: `602.000 * 0.10 / 1.000.000 = $0.0602`
- output: `25.500 * 0.40 / 1.000.000 = $0.0102`
- total por PDF: `$0.0704`
Coste máximo mensual:
- el límite mensual es 1.000 páginas y el límite por archivo es 100 páginas.
- el peor caso facturable son 10 PDFs al mes.
- `10 * $0.0704 = $0.704`
Precio mínimo recomendado sin pérdidas:
- **$0.99 / mes**
Precio FINAL:
- **$2.99 / mes**
Justificación:
- `$0.99` cubre el techo de coste LLM calculado con margen de seguridad de redondeo.
- bajar de `$0.70` dejaría el plan expuesto a pérdidas en el peor caso.
- Flash-Lite es significativamente más barato que Flash, lo que hace Premium el tier de mayor margen relativo.
### Pro
#### Modelo anterior vs modelo nuevo
El tier Pro cambió de `anthropic/claude-haiku-4.5` a `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash`.
Coste máximo por PDF con Claude Haiku 4.5 (modelo anterior):
- Precios Claude Haiku 4.5: input `$1.00 / 1M`, output `$5.00 / 1M`.
- input: `602.000 * 1.00 / 1.000.000 = $0.602`
- output: `75.500 * 5.00 / 1.000.000 = $0.3775`
- total por PDF: `$0.9795`
- peor caso mensual (4 PDFs): `4 * $0.9795 = $3.918`
- **Precio mínimo anterior: ~$4.99 / mes**
Coste máximo por PDF con Gemini 2.5 Flash (modelo actual):
- input: `602.000 * 0.30 / 1.000.000 = $0.1806`
- output: `75.500 * 2.50 / 1.000.000 = $0.18875`
- total por PDF: `$0.36935`
Coste máximo mensual:
- el límite mensual es 2.000 páginas y el límite por archivo es 500 páginas.
- el peor caso facturable son 4 PDFs al mes.
- `4 * $0.36935 = $1.4774`
Precio mínimo recomendado sin pérdidas:
- **$4.99 / mes**
PRECIO FINAL ELEGIDO:
- **$4.99 / mes**
Justificación:
- El cambio de Claude Haiku a Gemini 2.5 Flash reduce el coste máximo mensual de `$3.918` a `$1.4774`.
- Esto permite bajar el precio de Pro de `$4.99` a `$1.99` sin incurrir en pérdidas.
- `$1.99` es el primer escalón comercial simple que cubre el techo `$1.4774`.
- Gemini 2.5 Flash tiene output 2× más caro que Flash-Lite pero mantiene las capacidades multimodales a un coste por token muy inferior a Claude Haiku.
- Si se quisiera añadir margen para soporte, almacenamiento o Stripe, ese recargo debe sumarse encima de `$1.99`; no hace falta para cubrir solo inferencia.
## PAYG
Fórmula PAYG:
- `(coste proveedor LLM) * 1.40`
Notas:
- el catálogo PAYG queda restringido a Vertex (solo modelos Gemini).
- Premium y Pro no tiran de wallet si usan su modelo incluido; el wallet solo se usa cuando eligen otro modelo Vertex permitido.
## Privacidad y ciclo de vida
Paid tiers:
- flujo unificado multimodal sobre Vertex.
- no se usan modelos de Anthropic ni OpenAI.
Tras cancelación:
- se mantiene la política actual de retención de 90 días.
- durante la gracia, el usuario conserva sus decks y flashcards almacenadas.
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/finops_capacity_planning.md`
```md
# FinOps & Capacity Planning: PDF to Anki
This document projects the infrastructure and LLM costs for the `pdf_anki` module, demonstrating the economic viability of the "Frugal Fortress" architecture.
## 1. Baseline Assumptions
*   **1 Page** ≈ 500 words ≈ 666 tokens.
*   **Output per Page** ≈ 5 flashcards ≈ 250 tokens.
*   **Target Volume:** 10,000 pages processed per month.
*   **Total Input Tokens:** ~6.66 Million.
*   **Total Output Tokens:** ~2.50 Million.
## 2. LLM Provider Cost Comparison
We utilize dynamic routing based on the user's subscription tier. The table below compares the cost of processing 10,000 pages across our supported models.
| Provider / Model | Tier | Input Cost (per 1M) | Output Cost (per 1M) | Total Cost (10k Pages) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Groq** (`llama-3.3-70b`) | Free / Admin | $0.59 | $0.79 | **$5.90** |
| **Vertex AI** (`gemini-2.5-flash-lite`) | Premium / PAYG | $0.10 | $0.40 | **$1.66** |
| **Vertex AI** (`gemini-2.5-flash`) | Pro / PAYG | $0.30 | $2.50 | **$8.25** |
*Note: Prices based on official provider pricing as of April 2026, reflected in `src/app/core/pricing_registry.py`.*
## 3. Architectural Savings (The "Frugal" Impact)
Our architecture implements several layers of optimization that drastically reduce the theoretical costs above:
1. **Exact Hash Caching (L1):**
   *   *Mechanism:* `SHA-256` of the PDF bytes.
   *   *Impact:* If a university class of 100 students uploads the same syllabus, we process it **once**. Cost reduction: **99%** for viral documents.
2. **Semantic Deduplication (L2):**
   *   *Mechanism:* LanceDB vector search per chunk.
   *   *Impact:* If a chunk's embedding matches an existing chunk >95%, we skip LLM generation. Saves ~20% of output tokens on repetitive textbooks.
3. **Token Trimming:**
   *   *Mechanism:* `strip_headers_footers()` removes page numbers and repetitive headers before LLM inference.
   *   *Impact:* Saves ~5% of input tokens per document.
## 4. Profit Margin Analysis (Premium Tier)
*   **Premium Plan Price:** $2.99 / month.
*   **Limit:** 1,000 pages / month.
*   **Max LLM Cost (Gemini Flash-Lite):** $0.16 (Input) + $0.10 (Output) = **$0.26**.
*   **Gross Margin (Compute only):** **91.3%**.
By leveraging Vertex AI's Flash-Lite model for the Premium tier, we maintain multimodal capabilities (processing images/tables in PDFs) while preserving SaaS-level profit margins.
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/CONFIGURATION.md`
```md
# Configuration Guide: PDF to Anki
This document outlines the environment variables required to configure dynamic routing, FinOps pricing, and DSPy pilots for the PDF to Anki module.
## 1. Dynamic Tier Routing
The system dynamically routes PDF processing based on the user's subscription tier:
*   **Free / Admin:** `groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile` (Chunked Pipeline)
*   **Premium:** `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash-lite` (Unified Multimodal Pipeline)
*   **Pro:** `gemini/gemini-2.5-flash` (Unified Multimodal Pipeline)
*   **PAYG (Pay-As-You-Go):** Defaults to `PDF_ANKI_UNIFIED_MODEL_PAYG`, allowing users to select from a restricted list of Vertex AI models.
## 2. FinOps Pricing Configuration
Prices are loaded into the `PricingRegistry` via `.env` variables. They must be formatted as a JSON array: `["input_price_per_1M", "output_price_per_1M", "audio_price_per_1M"]`.
```env
# Example Pricing Configuration
PRICE_GROQ_LLAMA_3_3_70B_VERSATILE='["0.59", "0.79", "0.00"]'
PRICE_GEMINI_GEMINI_2_5_FLASH_LITE='["0.10", "0.40", "0.30"]'
PRICE_GEMINI_GEMINI_2_5_FLASH='["0.30", "2.50", "1.00"]'
```
## 3. Required API Keys
| Variable | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `LLAMAPARSE_PDF2ANKI_API_KEY` | LlamaParse extraction for Free/Admin tiers. |
| `GROQ_PDF2ANKI_API_KEY` | Groq inference for Free/Admin flashcard generation. |
| `VERTEX_PROJECT_ID` | Required for Premium/Pro/PAYG multimodal processing. |
## 4. DSPy Pilot Configuration
The backend can dynamically inject optimized instructions into the base prompt using compiled DSPy artifacts, without altering the dynamic Few-Shot memory.
```env
# DSPy Feature Flags
DSPY_ENABLED=true
DSPY_PDF_ANKI_ENABLED=true
DSPY_PDF_ANKI_ARTIFACT_PATH=config/dspy/pdf_anki.json
DSPY_PDF_ANKI_ROLLOUT_PERCENT=100
DSPY_PDF_ANKI_TENANT_ALLOWLIST=tenant_1,tenant_2
```
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/EXECUTIVE_SUMMARY.md`
```md
# Executive Summary: The Frugal Fortress Architecture
## The Challenge
Scaling Generative AI applications typically results in skyrocketing infrastructure costs and severe compliance nightmares (SOC2/GDPR/HIPAA). Traditional microservice architectures introduce unnecessary network latency, while naive LLM integrations expose sensitive corporate data (PII/PHI) to third-party providers, violating enterprise security policies.
## The Solution: "Frugal Fortress"
We engineered a **Modular Monolith** in Python (FastAPI) and Next.js, specifically designed for heavy document processing, financial extraction, and AI generation. It operates under a strict "Frugal AI" philosophy, guaranteeing data privacy and high availability while maintaining a baseline infrastructure cost of just $5/month.
### Core Pillars
1. **FinOps & Cost Predictability:**
   * **Edge Compute Offloading:** Image compression and basic validations are pushed to the browser (WASM/Canvas), reducing backend bandwidth and VLM token consumption by up to 95%.
   * **Multi-Tier Semantic Caching:** Reduces LLM costs by up to 80%. If a document or concept has been processed before, the system serves it from a Redis L1 Cache (Exact Hash) or a LanceDB L2 Cache (Semantic Deduplication) in <50ms, bypassing the LLM entirely.
   * **Dynamic Routing:** Intelligently routes simple tasks to fast, free-tier models (e.g., Groq Llama-3.3) and complex tasks to premium models (e.g., Vertex AI Gemini), maximizing ROI.
2. **Enterprise-Grade Security (SOC2 Ready):**
   * **Zero Data Retention:** Paid tiers utilize Google Vertex AI under strict enterprise agreements ensuring customer data is *never* used to train foundational models.
   * **Fail-Secure DLP Proxy:** A local, CPU-bound Small Language Model (Llama 3.2 3B) and Microsoft Presidio intercept and redact all PII/PHI *before* it leaves the Virtual Private Cloud (VPC).
   * **Application-Level Encryption (ALE):** All sensitive data at rest (Flashcards, Transcripts, Receipts) is encrypted via AES-128-CBC (Fernet) before hitting the database.
3. **Site Reliability Engineering (SRE):**
   * **Async Burst Ingestion:** Heavy parsing (like 500-page PDFs) is offloaded to idempotent background workers (`Arq`), ensuring the main FastAPI Event Loop is never blocked during traffic spikes.
   * **Distributed Circuit Breakers:** Redis-backed circuit breakers detect LLM provider outages and automatically trigger "Graceful Degradation" (fallback to secondary models).
   * **Async SingleFlight:** Prevents cache stampedes during viral traffic spikes by coalescing identical concurrent requests into a single LLM execution.
## Key Performance Metrics
* **Latency:** < 3 seconds for standard generation; < 50ms for cache hits.
* **Uptime:** 99.9% target, sustained via automated multi-provider fallbacks.
* **Security:** 0% PII leakage to external APIs.
* **Self-Correction:** The "Nested Learning Loop" improves model accuracy in real-time via user feedback, achieving fine-tuning results at zero training cost.
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/SUPPORT_TICKETS_ARCHITECTURE.md`
```md
# Support Tickets Architecture (Web Helpdesk)
## 1. Architectural Overview
The PDF to Anki module integrates with the Frugal Fortress native Helpdesk system. Instead of relying on external SaaS tools (like Zendesk), users can open support tickets directly from the Next.js frontend.
This system is built on strict **Hexagonal Architecture**, sharing the core support domain with other modules while using a REST API adapter for the web frontend.
## 2. Attachment Storage & Security
Users can attach screenshots or error logs to their tickets to help debug PDF parsing issues.
* **Limits:** Max 10 attachments per message. Max 5MB per file. Max 25MB total per request.
* **Security:** Files are sanitized, and MIME types are strictly validated against an allowlist (`image/jpeg`, `image/png`, `application/pdf`, etc.) to prevent malicious uploads.
* **Storage:** Attachments are uploaded to an S3-compatible object storage (or local disk in dev) via the `SupportAttachmentStoragePort`.
## 3. Rate Limiting & Abuse Prevention
To prevent spam, the support endpoints are protected by the Redis Token Bucket middleware:
* **Creation:** Limited to 6 tickets per minute (with a burst of 3 per 10 seconds).
* **Replies:** Limited to 10 replies per minute.
* **Business Logic:** A user can only have a maximum of 3 *active* tickets at any given time.
## 4. Admin REST API Integration
Support agents interact with the system via the secure REST API (`/admin/tickets`).
* When an admin replies via `POST /admin/tickets/{ticket_id}/reply`, the conversation history is updated in Redis (`ticket:active:{ticket_id}`).
* The user sees the response in real-time upon refreshing their web dashboard.
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/PAYG_WALLET_AND_BILLING.md`
```md
# Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG) Wallet & Dynamic Billing
## 1. FinOps Philosophy
Processing massive corporate documents (e.g., 500-page PDFs) can easily trigger "Denial of Wallet" scenarios if users select expensive models like `gemini-2.5-pro`.
To support high-volume Enterprise users safely, the `pdf_anki` module implements a prepaid **PAYG Wallet** with a strict Two-Phase Commit billing architecture.
## 2. Dynamic Model Selection & Fallback
PAYG users can dynamically switch their unified multimodal model directly from the Next.js frontend.
* The backend fetches the available models and their costs from the `PricingRegistry`.
* **Graceful Degradation:** If a user selects an expensive model but their wallet balance is insufficient to cover the *estimated* cost of the PDF, the system does not crash with a 500 error. Instead, it intercepts the request, logs a `pdf_anki_payg_balance_insufficient_fallback` event, and gracefully degrades the processing pipeline to the user's base tier (e.g., the Free LlamaParse + Groq stack).
## 3. The Two-Phase Deduction Lifecycle (2PC)
To prevent race conditions during concurrent PDF uploads, billing is handled transactionally:
1. **Phase 1 (Pre-flight Estimate):** Before processing, `estimate_unified_pdf_token_budget` calculates the maximum possible tokens based on the PDF byte size and the requested flashcard limit. The `BudgetManager` reserves this USD amount in Redis.
2. **Phase 2 (Inference):** The PDF is processed via Vertex AI (Unified Pipeline) or Groq (Chunked Pipeline).
3. **Phase 3 (Exact Deduction):** The `LLMResponse` returns the *exact* tokens used. The backend calculates the final USD cost, applies the 40% markup (`PDF_ANKI_WALLET_CHARGE_MULTIPLIER`), and deducts it from the PostgreSQL `pdf_anki_users` table using a `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` row-level lock.
4. **Audit Trail:** A `WalletTransaction` record is inserted, providing an immutable ledger of credits and debits.
## 4. Stripe Webhook Integration
When a user tops up their balance via the Stripe Customer Portal:
1. The `checkout.session.completed` webhook is intercepted.
2. The payload signature is cryptographically verified.
3. The USD amount is converted to a Decimal, added to the user's `balance_usd`, and a `CREDIT` transaction is logged.
4. The user's `payg` flag is set to `True`, instantly unlocking the premium model selector in the UI.
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/README.md`
```md
# Module: PDF to Anki (Async Knowledge Extraction)
## 1. Architectural Overview
The PDF to Anki module is a heavy-compute, asynchronous pipeline designed to ingest large documents and generate spaced-repetition flashcards. It is built for high concurrency, ensuring the FastAPI Event Loop is never blocked by CPU-bound tasks.
**Core Flow:**
1. **Upload & Enqueue:** The frontend uploads a PDF. The API validates tier limits, encrypts the raw bytes into Redis (1-hour TTL), enqueues an `Arq` background job, and immediately returns an HTTP 202 Accepted.
2. **Dual-Pipeline Processing:**
   * *Chunked Pipeline (Free/Admin):* LlamaParse extracts Markdown -> Semantic Chunking -> LanceDB Vector Search (Deduplication) -> Groq (Llama-3.3) generates cards per chunk.
   * *Unified Pipeline (Premium/Pro/PAYG):* The raw PDF bytes are sent directly to Vertex AI (Gemini 2.5 Flash) for holistic, multimodal flashcard generation.
3. **Persistence:** The generated deck is encrypted via ALE and stored in PostgreSQL, while a cached copy is placed in Redis for instant retrieval.
## 2. FinOps & Performance Optimizations
* **L1 Exact Hash Cache:** If the `SHA-256` hash of the uploaded PDF matches an existing processed document, the system instantly returns the cached deck, saving 100% of the LLM costs.
* **L2 Semantic Deduplication:** In the chunked pipeline, each text chunk is embedded and queried against `LanceDB`. If a highly similar concept (>95%) already exists in the vector store, LLM generation for that specific chunk is skipped.
* **Token Trimming:** A pure Python optimizer (`strip_headers_footers`) removes repetitive page numbers and headers before LLM inference, reducing input token costs.
## 3. DSPy Integration (Prompt Optimization)
This module supports an experimental DSPy pilot. When enabled, compiled heuristic policies dynamically adjust the System Prompt based on the user's tier and the document's complexity, optimizing for "high-signal, exam-worthy" cards without altering the underlying Python domain logic.
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/DEMO docs/DEMO.md`
```md
INCOGNITAS:
- podemos montar un kwnoledge graph (Neo4jGraph) a partir de los chunks de un PDF? (usando cypher) ? Curso RAG, Leccion 4
UAT (User Acceptance Testing) Sign-off o Technical Showcase.
🛠️ 1. Software Complementario (El Stack de "Show, Don't Tell")
Durante la Live Demo, no muestres solo la interfaz de usuario (Next.js). Debes tener pestañas abiertas con las siguientes herramientas (todas presentes en tu docker-compose.yml y pyproject.toml):
- Grafana + Prometheus + Loki (Observabilidad de Infraestructura):
Qué mostrar: Un dashboard en tiempo real mostrando el uso de CPU/RAM, el estado del DB_POOL_CHECKED_OUT (conexiones a Postgres) y el contador de tareas en background (BACKGROUND_TASKS_ACTIVE).
El Pitch: "Aquí vemos cómo el sistema respira. Si hay un pico de tráfico, el CpuBoundExecutor encola las tareas sin bloquear el Event Loop de FastAPI."
- Langfuse o Arize Phoenix (Observabilidad de IA & FinOps):
Qué mostrar:
**- **Diferencia de Pipelines (Free vs Paid):
    Traza Free: Muestra múltiples llamadas pequeñas a Groq (Llama-3.3) iterando sobre los chunks de texto.
    Traza Premium/Pro: Muestra una única llamada masiva multimodal a Vertex AI (Gemini) enviando los bytes del PDF directamente.
**- Deduplicación Semántica (L2 Cache):
búsqueda vectorial. En src/app/modules/pdf_anki/application/process_pdf.py, el sistema divide el PDF en chunks (párrafos). Por cada chunk, genera un embedding y consulta a LanceDB.
Si LanceDB devuelve un chunk previo con una "distancia" menor a SIMILARITY_THRESHOLD = 0.05 (es decir, una similitud > 95%), el sistema asume que ese concepto ya fue procesado y se salta la llamada al LLM (Groq/Vertex) para ahorrar tokens.
Debes capturar el log donde el sistema evalúa un chunk contra LanceDB y decide saltarse la generación del LLM porque el concepto ya existe en el mazo (ahorro masivo de tokens de salida).
Debes buscar en Loki o en tu terminal:
"event": "semantic_cache_hit_skipping_llm"
Usar archivo test_dedup.pdf
Resultado esperado: El sistema generará flashcards para la Sección 1 y 2. Al llegar a la Sección 3, el embedding coincidirá con la Sección 1, emitirá el log semantic_cache_hit_skipping_llm y no gastará tokens de salida.
**DSPy
Añadir en .env:
DSPY_ENABLED=true
DSPY_PDF_ANKI_ENABLED=true
-
- Locust (Pruebas de Carga / Chaos Engineering):
Qué mostrar: La interfaz web de Locust (http://localhost:8089) inyectando 50 usuarios concurrentes subiendo PDFs.
El Pitch: "El papel lo aguanta todo, pero veamos cómo se comporta el sistema bajo estrés real."
- Swagger UI / ReDoc (/docs):
Qué mostrar: Los contratos estrictos de la API, los esquemas Pydantic y los códigos de error documentados (429, 422, 206).
El Pitch: "Nuestra API es auto-documentada y fuertemente tipada. Los clientes móviles o web tienen contratos garantizados."
📄 2. Documentos de Respaldo (Los Artefactos Enterprise)
Para acompañar la solución, debes entregar un paquete documental conciso pero denso en valor arquitectónico:
- Diagramas del Modelo C4 (Context, Container, Component):
con Mermaid.js. Muestra claramente los límites de la Arquitectura Hexagonal (Puertos y Adaptadores).
- ADRs (Architecture Decision Records):
Documentos cortos que explican por qué tomaste ciertas decisiones.
Ejemplo ADR 1: "¿Por qué usamos Postgres + pgvector en lugar de Pinecone?" (Respuesta: Reducción de latencia de red, simplificación del stack, coste cero adicional).
Ejemplo ADR 2: "¿Por qué usamos un SLM local (Llama 3.2 3B) para el DLP Proxy?" (Respuesta: Cumplimiento SOC2, los datos PII nunca tocan APIs externas).
- Matriz de FinOps y Capacity Planning:
Una hoja de cálculo (Excel/Sheets) proyectando los costes. Ej: "Procesar 10,000 páginas de PDF al mes cuesta $X usando Groq (Llama 3.3) vs $Y usando Vertex AI (Gemini 2.5 Flash)".
- Security & Compliance Sheet (SOC2/GDPR):
Un documento de una página detallando el ciclo de vida del dato: Encriptación en reposo (Supabase CLE), encriptación a nivel de aplicación (Fernet/AES para las flashcards), y el enmascaramiento de PII (Presidio).
📦 3. ¿Cómo se entrega al Cliente / Stakeholder?
La entrega debe ser un "Leave-Behind Package" (Paquete para llevar) que el cliente pueda revisar por su cuenta tras la reunión:
- Executive Summary (PDF de 2 páginas):
Problema de negocio resuelto, ROI esperado, resumen de la arquitectura (Frugal Fortress) y métricas clave de rendimiento (Latencia < 3s, 99.9% Uptime).
Acceso al Repositorio (GitHub/GitLab):
Un repositorio inmaculado. El README.md debe ser una obra de arte (como el que ya tienes estructurado).
One-Click Quickstart:
El cliente debe poder levantar todo el sistema en su máquina con un solo comando: docker compose up -d. Si requiere 15 pasos de configuración, perderás su interés. Proporciona un .env.example claro.
- Postman Collection / Bruno Workspace:
Una colección pre-configurada con variables de entorno para que sus ingenieros puedan probar la API inmediatamente sin escribir código.
💼 4. ¿Qué documentar para tu Portafolio Profesional?
Para un portafolio de nivel Staff/Principal Engineer, debes alejarte del típico "Hice una app con Next.js y Python". Debes enfocarte en Sistemas, Resiliencia y Liderazgo Técnico.
Crea un caso de estudio (Case Study) en tu web o GitHub Pages estructurado así:
El Desafío (The Challenge): "Construir un sistema de procesamiento de documentos pesados (PDFs) y generación de IA que escale a miles de usuarios con un presupuesto de infraestructura de $5/mes (Frugal AI)."
La Arquitectura (The Architecture):
Muestra el diagrama del Monolito Modular. Explica por qué elegiste un monolito bien estructurado sobre microservicios prematuros (reducción de latencia, despliegue simplificado, compartición de recursos).
Destaca la Arquitectura Hexagonal: Muestra un fragmento de código de cómo el dominio (process_pdf_use_case) es completamente agnóstico de si el LLM es Groq, OpenAI o Vertex.
Patrones de Resiliencia (Resilience Patterns):
Documenta tu implementación del Circuit Breaker en Redis. Explica cómo el sistema hace Fallback automático a modelos más baratos o rápidos si el proveedor principal cae.
Muestra el manejo de Idempotencia en los workers de fondo (Arq) para evitar procesar el mismo PDF dos veces y gastar tokens innecesariamente.
El "Nested Learning Loop" (Innovación en IA):
Esta es tu "Killer Feature". Documenta cómo el sistema captura el feedback explícito del usuario (cuando editan una flashcard), lo vectoriza, y lo inyecta dinámicamente como ejemplos Few-Shot en futuros prompts. Explica cómo esto mejora la precisión del modelo en tiempo real sin el coste de hacer Fine-Tuning.
- Zero-Compromise Testing:
Muestra capturas de pantalla de tu pipeline de CI/CD. Destaca el uso de Mutation Testing (mutmut) y la evaluación semántica con DeepEval (LLM-as-a-judge). Esto grita "Ingeniería de Software de Alta Madurez".
Resumen para el Portafolio:
"No soy solo un programador que conecta APIs de IA. Soy un Arquitecto de Sistemas que diseña plataformas de IA seguras (SOC2), económicamente viables (FinOps) y tolerantes a fallos (SRE)."
🎥 1. Software Recomendado para la Grabación
Para un portafolio de nivel Staff/Principal, necesitas un acabado impecable. Olvida grabar directamente con Zoom o Teams.
A. Grabación de Pantalla (Screen Recording):
OBS Studio (Windows/Linux/macOS): La alternativa gratuita y profesional. Configura "Escenas" para cambiar fluidamente entre tu cara (cámara), el navegador, la terminal y Grafana.
Loom (Plan Pro): Si necesitas enviarlo de forma asíncrona rápidamente, pero descárgalo en 4K para editarlo después.
B. Entorno de Demostración (El Escenario):
Terminal: Usa Warp o iTerm2 (Mac) / Windows Terminal con un prompt limpio (ej. Starship o Oh My Zsh). Aumenta el tamaño de la fuente a 14-16pt. Nadie quiere entrecerrar los ojos para leer tus logs.
Navegador: Crea un Perfil Limpio en Chrome/Edge. Sin marcadores personales, sin extensiones visibles (salvo las necesarias).
IDE: VS Code o Cursor en modo "Zen" (sin barra de actividades lateral) con un tema oscuro profesional (ej. Vercel Theme o GitHub Dark).
C. Edición (Post-Producción):
Adobe Premiere. Añade subtítulos si tu audiencia es global.
## 📜 2. Detailed Script & Diagram Mapping (The "Staff Engineer" Pitch)
*Ideal duration: 8 to 12 minutes.*
*Golden Rule: Tell them what you are going to show, show it, then tell them what you just showed.*
**🎬 Act 1: The Hook & Architecture (Minute 0:00 - 2:00)**
*   **Visual:** Open `docs/diagrams/backend_monolith_context.md` and `docs/diagrams/hexagonal_module.md`.
*   **The Pitch:**
    "Scaling Generative AI usually skyrockets infrastructure costs and creates SOC2 compliance nightmares. Today, I'll show you the PDF to Anki module of the 'Frugal Fortress'.
    As you can see in our Hexagonal Module diagram, the core domain is completely isolated. We can swap Groq for Vertex AI without touching a single line of business logic. This guarantees data privacy and operates with a baseline infrastructure cost of just $5/month."
**🎬 Act 2: The "Happy Path" and Async UX (Minute 2:00 - 4:30)**
*   **Visual:** Browser with Next.js UI (`/pdf-anki`) + `docs/diagrams/PDF2ANKI/diagram_pdf_anki.md` (Sequence Diagram).
*   **Action:** Upload a real PDF (e.g., a 5-page technical manual).
*   **The Pitch:**
    "In the frontend, we never block the user. Uploading returns a 202 Accepted instantly.
    Looking at our Sequence Diagram, you can see the Arq worker taking over. It extracts text, performs semantic chunking, and generates flashcards. Notice the dual-pipeline: Free users go through LlamaParse and Groq, while Paid users go directly to Vertex AI for multimodal processing with Zero Data Retention."
**🎬 Act 3: Under the Hood - Observability & FinOps (Minute 4:30 - 7:00)**
*   **Visual:** Grafana Dashboards + `docs/diagrams/infrastructure_view.md` + `docs/diagrams/finops_lifecycle.md`.
*   **The Pitch:**
    "An Enterprise system doesn't operate blind. Our Infrastructure View shows the complete OpenTelemetry stack (Promtail, Loki, Tempo, Prometheus).
    For FinOps, look at the FinOps Lifecycle diagram. Every LLM call is intercepted by a Lua script in Redis that reserves and commits budget atomically. If a prompt becomes inefficient, we detect the exact USD cost here before it impacts the monthly bill."
**🎬 Act 4: Chaos Engineering & Resilience (Minute 7:00 - 9:00)**
*   **Visual:** Split terminal (Worker logs vs Chaos script) + `docs/diagrams/circuit_breaker_state.md` + `docs/diagrams/concurrency_singleflight.md`.
*   **Action:** Execute `chaos_rate_limit.py` or simulate a Groq 429 error.
*   **The Pitch:**
    "What happens when the AI provider fails? In a naive app, it crashes.
    As shown in our Circuit Breaker State Machine, our Redis-backed breaker detects the failure and triggers a 'Graceful Degradation' to a cheaper/faster secondary model.
    Furthermore, if 100 students upload the same viral PDF simultaneously, our `AsyncSingleFlight` pattern (shown here) coalesces the requests. Only one LLM call is made; the other 99 get the cached result. 100% cost savings."
**🎬 Act 5: The "Nested Learning Loop" (AI Innovation) (Minute 9:00 - 10:30)**
*   **Visual:** Next.js UI + `docs/diagrams/database_schema.md` + `docs/diagrams/rag_pipeline.md`.
*   **Action:** Edit a generated flashcard to fix a formatting error and click "Save".
*   **The Pitch:**
    "Fine-tuning is expensive and slow. We use RAG for self-correction.
    When I edit this card, the backend vectorizes the correction and saves it to `pgvector` (as seen in our Database Schema).
    The next time this user uploads a PDF, our RAG Pipeline retrieves this correction and injects it into the System Prompt as a Few-Shot example. The system learns the user's style in real-time, at zero training cost."
**🎬 Act 6: Security (SOC2) & Close (Minute 10:30 - 12:00)**
*   **Visual:** IDE showing `pii_scrubber.py` and `feedback_privacy.py`.
*   **The Pitch:**
    "For Fortune 500 clients, privacy is non-negotiable. We scrub PII before it hits external LLMs.
    We also apply Application-Level Encryption (ALE). Even a DBA with access to Postgres cannot read the users' flashcards.
    This is the Frugal Fortress: designed not just to work, but to scale securely, auditably, and with predictable economics. Thank you."
💡 Consejos Pro para la Grabación:
Aumenta el tamaño de la fuente: Todo el código y las terminales deben estar al menos a 16pt. Si se ve pequeño en un móvil, perderás a los reclutadores que revisan LinkedIn en el teléfono.
Usa datos reales pero anonimizados: Sube un PDF que parezca un documento corporativo real (un contrato de prueba, un manual de AWS), no un "Lorem Ipsum".
No programes en vivo: La demo es para mostrar el sistema funcionando y su arquitectura, no tus habilidades de mecanografía. Ten los scripts preparados (make eval, python chaos.py) y ejecútalos con confianza.
🚀 PARTE 1: Más allá del Vídeo (La Estrategia del Staff Engineer)
Publicar el vídeo de la DEMO (UAT) en LinkedIn es el gancho perfecto, pero para cerrar entrevistas de alto nivel, necesitas que tu ecosistema grite "Madurez Técnica". Esto es lo que debes añadir:
1. El "Whitepaper" (Tu README.md):
Los CTOs no leen código fuente de buenas a primeras; leen documentación de arquitectura. Tu README.md no debe decir "Cómo instalar esto". Debe ser un mini-Whitepaper:
El Problema: "Escalar IA generativa destruye los márgenes de beneficio y expone datos sensibles."
La Solución: "Frugal Fortress: Un monolito modular con RAG auto-corregible, FinOps integrado y cumplimiento SOC2."
Métricas de Impacto: "Reducción del 80% en costes de LLM mediante Semantic Caching. 0% de fugas de PII."
2. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs):
Crea una carpeta docs/adr/. Escribe 3 o 4 documentos cortos (1 página) explicando por qué tomaste decisiones difíciles.
Ejemplo: 001-use-modular-monolith-over-microservices.md
Ejemplo: 002-implement-circuit-breaker-for-llm-fallback.md
Por qué funciona: Demuestra que no sigues modas (Hype Driven Development), sino que tomas decisiones basadas en trade-offs (coste, latencia, mantenimiento).
3. El "Live Sandbox" (Controlado):
Si puedes, despliega el frontend en Vercel y el backend en Railway. Pero protégelo. Usa tu propio sistema de Rate Limiting y BudgetManager para que los reclutadores puedan jugar con él sin arruinarte. Que vean el banner de "Límite de capacidad alcanzado" es en realidad una feature que demuestra que tu sistema FinOps funciona.
🧩 PARTE 2: Integrando pdf-anki en genui (El Meta-Portafolio)
El módulo genui es tu comercial de ventas autónomo. Cuando un CTO escriba en el input: "Somos una empresa de salud (HIPAA) y necesitamos procesar miles de PDFs médicos de forma barata", tu backend debe hacer streaming de una propuesta que mezcle texto generado por IA con componentes reales de tu proyecto.
Aquí tienes los componentes exactos de pdf-anki que debes reutilizar y los que debes generar dinámicamente:
A. Componentes Estáticos (Reutilizados de tu código actual)
Estos son componentes que ya tienes construidos y que genui renderizará para demostrar "lo que ya has construido".
PdfAnkiFlashcard (El Valor de Negocio):
Uso en GenUI: Cuando el LLM detecte que el cliente necesita "extracción de conocimiento", el stream devuelve {"type": "component", "component": "PdfAnkiFlashcard", "props": {"front": "Concepto Médico", "back": "Definición extraída del PDF", "tags": ["HIPAA", "Demo"]}}.
Impacto: El reclutador ve una tarjeta interactiva real (que se voltea al hacer clic) incrustada en medio de la propuesta de texto.
PdfAnkiErrorAlert (La Resiliencia / SRE):
Uso en GenUI: Para demostrar cómo manejas fallos. El LLM puede decir: "Nuestros sistemas están diseñados para fallar elegantemente. Así es como se ve cuando un proveedor de IA se cae:" y renderiza tu componente de alerta con el código groq_free_tier_busy o provider_rate_limit.
Impacto: Demuestra madurez en UX y manejo de errores (Graceful Degradation).
CapacityBanner (El Control FinOps):
Uso en GenUI: "Controlamos los costes de infraestructura al milímetro. Los usuarios reciben avisos antes de saturar la base de datos:" -> Renderiza el banner de capacidad al 80%.
B. Componentes Dinámicos (Generados en vivo por GenUI)
Estos son componentes exclusivos de genui que el LLM parametriza en tiempo real basándose en el prompt del reclutador.
SecurityComplianceBadge (El Sello SOC2/HIPAA):
Cómo funciona: Si el reclutador menciona "datos sensibles", el LLM hace streaming de este componente.
Props: {"standard": "SOC2", "status": "Compliant", "features": ["PII Scrubbing via Presidio", "Application-Level Encryption (AES)"]}.
FinOpsROICalculator (El Argumento de Venta):
Cómo funciona: Un componente visual que compara costes.
Props generadas por IA: Si el cliente dice "procesamos 100,000 páginas", el LLM calcula y renderiza un gráfico de barras: {"competitorCost": "$1500", "frugalFortressCost": "$45", "savings": "97%"}.
ArchitectureFitScore (El Cierre):
Cómo funciona: Un medidor circular (tipo velocímetro).
Props: {"score": 98, "reason": "La arquitectura Hexagonal encaja perfectamente con su necesidad de cambiar de LLMs sin refactorizar el core."}
💡 Resumen para tu Portafolio / Mentalidad
En mi arquitectura, Producción es sagrada. La DEMO que están viendo se ejecuta en un entorno de Staging idéntico a Producción. ¿Por qué? Porque para demostrar la verdadera resiliencia de este sistema (Circuit Breakers, Rate Limits, PII Scrubbing), necesito someterlo a estrés extremo y simular caídas de proveedores de IA. Hacemos el 'Chaos Engineering' y obtenemos la aprobación del cliente (UAT) en Staging. Una vez aprobado, el despliegue a Producción es un simple cambio de etiquetas de infraestructura, garantizando cero tiempo de inactividad (Zero-Downtime) y cero sorpresas.
En la GenUI
Construí un Monolito Modular en Python y Next.js enfocado en FinOps y SRE. Pero lo más interesante es cómo lo presento: construí un módulo llamado GenUI que actúa como mi ingeniero pre-venta. Si le dices a la aplicación qué tipo de empresa eres, el backend analiza tu contexto, evalúa el 'Fit' arquitectónico, y hace streaming en tiempo real de una propuesta comercial que incrusta componentes React 100% funcionales de mis otros proyectos (como tarjetas de estudio generadas por RAG o alertas de Circuit Breakers) para demostrar visualmente que el código hace exactamente lo que promete.
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/DEMO docs/script_architecture_deep_dive.md`
```md
# docs/pdf-anki/DEMO docs/script_architecture_deep_dive.md
# The Staff Engineer Pitch: PDF to Anki (Live Demo Script)
*Target Audience: CTOs, VPs of Engineering, Enterprise Architects.*
*Tone: Authoritative, FinOps-focused, Security-conscious.*
**Tasks:**
- Enable `DEBUG=false` in `.env` to ensure JSON structured logging.
- Enable `DSPY_ENABLED=true` and `DSPY_PDF_ANKI_ENABLED=true` in `.env`.
- Restart the API and worker before recording so the Phoenix/OpenTelemetry privacy config is active.
- For Act 2, use PDFs that are not already in the L1 deck cache. For Act 3, intentionally reuse the exact same PDF bytes with a second user.
---
## Act 1: The Hook & Hexagonal Decoupling (0:00 - 2:00)
**(Visual: Open `docs/architecture/WHITEPAPER.md` briefly, then switch to  `docs/diagrams/modules/pdf_anki/c4_model.md` in the IDE. Highlight the Component Diagram showing the "Dependency Inversion", then switch to `docs/architecture/HEXAGONAL_DECOUPLING.md` and finally the Next.js UI)**
[CAMARA]
"Scaling Generative AI for heavy document processing usually skyrockets infrastructure costs and creates SOC2 compliance nightmares.
[PRESENTACION]
Today, I will introduce you to Kera (Knowledge Extraction & Retention Architecture), the PDF to Anki AI Copilot module of our 'Frugal Fortress'.
As outlined in our **Architecture Whitepaper**, we avoided latency-heavy microservices in favor of a highly optimized **Modular Monolith**. Kera operates as a strictly isolated bounded context within this system.
If we look at the **C4 Component Diagram** and our **Hexagonal Decoupling** documentation, you can see how the core domain is completely isolated. We can swap Groq for Vertex AI, or LlamaParse for a local OCR, without touching a single line of business logic. This guarantees data privacy and allows us to operate with a baseline infrastructure cost of just $5/month. Let's see it in action."
## Act 2: The Clash of Pipelines (Chunked vs Multimodal) (2:00 - 4:00)
**(Action: Upload a 5-page PDF with a Free user. Then upload a 100-page technical manual with a Pro user.)**
**(Visual: Open `docs/diagrams/modules/pdf_anki/diagram_pdf_anki.md` (Async RAG Flow), then switch to Arize Phoenix `http://localhost:6006`, select all(not Root Spans) and filter by 'name == 'worker.process_pdf' to show the traces, y despues de la prueba haz clic en la traza para mostrar el arbol de llamadas al LLM, y haz click en una de esas llamadas y ve a la pestaña de atributos para mostrar datos(free): model_name, chunk_index, chunk_chars, cost_usd )**
"In the frontend, we never block the user. Uploading returns a 202 Accepted instantly. But under the hood, as shown in our **Async RAG Flow diagram**, we execute a dual-pipeline architecture.
If we look at our AI Observability tool, Arize Phoenix, you'll notice the Free Tier trace shows a tree with multiple child spans—one Groq call per text chunk. This minimizes memory usage but increases latency. Notice that sensitive payloads are intentionally opaque in the trace: user identifiers are redacted, and LLM prompts, outputs, and raw document bytes are hidden. We keep the operational metadata that matters: model, tier, token counts, latency, chunk index and chunk chars, among others.
Now look at the Pro Tier trace. The key span is `pdf_anki.llm.vertex_multimodal_pdf` (and attributes: model name, pdf_anki.pdf_bytes y pdf_anki.pdf_base64_chars_est, privacy.zero_data_retention_route): a single, massive multimodal ingest call to Vertex AI. We send the raw PDF bytes directly in Base64, but Phoenix only shows safe metadata like PDF size and estimated Base64 footprint. Vertex AI processes the document context in one shot, maintaining context cohesion and, crucially, routing Enterprise workloads through our Zero Data Retention path."
## Act 3: Global Idempotency (L1 Exact Cache) (4:00 - 5:00)
**(Action: Upload the exact same PDF bytes first with User A, then with User B.)**
**(Visual: Show `src/app/modules/pdf_anki/router.py` around `compute_file_hash` / `get_by_file_hash`. Then switch to Arize Phoenix and show the trace for User B's upload (worker.process_pdf). Finally, switch to Grafana/Loki and show the query `{job="app"} |= "pdf_exact_cache_hit"`.)**
"Before we even talk about semantic deduplication, we apply a stronger invariant: global idempotency by file hash.
If we look at the router code, the API computes a SHA-256 hash of the raw PDF bytes before enqueueing the job. If another user uploads the exact same file, we do not pay the LLM again.
Look at the Arize Phoenix trace for this second upload. The absence of Groq or Vertex child spans is the feature here. The worker started, checked the cache, and stopped. There is zero inference happening.
If we switch to our Loki logs, we see exactly why: a `pdf_exact_cache_hit`. The existing document identity was reused, the worker hit the encrypted L1 deck cache, and we created a fresh deck and encrypted flashcards owned by the new requesting user.
This gives us cross-user compute reuse without cross-user data leakage. The expensive AI work happens once, while tenancy remains strict at the database row level."
## Act 4: Semantic Deduplication (L2 Cache) (5:00 - 6:30)
**(Action:. May need to execute 'uv run python scripts/lancedb_drop_pdf_anki_chunks.py' just in case.**
    "Global idempotency is great for exact duplicates, but how do we handle slightly modified documents? Imagine a user uploads a revised version of a textbook. The file hash changes, so our L1 cache misses. But we don't want to pay the LLM to summarize the exact same paragraphs again.
 **Set chunk limit to 500 characters on the file config/runtime_tunable.json**
    To demonstrate this, I'm going to force the system to use smaller chunks. Instead of restarting containers or running a CI/CD pipeline, I'll just modify our `runtime_tunable.json` file. Our `DynamicConfigService` detects the file change and updates the asynchronous workers in milliseconds with zero downtime.
    Now, I'll upload version 1 of our document.
**(Action: Upload `doc_v1.pdf`. Wait for it to complete. Then upload `doc_v2.pdf` which contains the same text plus one new paragraph.)**
**(Visual: Open `docs/adr/0009-postgres-pgvector-vs-pinecone.md`, then switch to Arize Phoenix (POST /api/v1/pdf/upload) and open the trace for `doc_v2.pdf`. Finally, switch to Grafana/Loki and run the query `{job="app"} |= "semantic_cache_hit_skipping_llm"`.)**
    I just uploaded version 2 of a document. The worker chunked the text into 5 segments. But if we look at the Arize Phoenix trace, you'll notice there are only 3 LLM calls (for chunks 0, 3, and 4). Why did we skip chunks 1 and 2?"
**(Acción: Cambia a Grafana Loki y muestra los logs de semantic_stitching_applied)**
    "Let's look at our Loki logs. As outlined in ADR-0009, we implemented an L2 Semantic Cache using LanceDB. For chunks 1 and 2, the system detected a >98% semantic similarity with vectors from the previous document.
    It emitted a semantic_stitching_applied event and completely bypassed the AI provider. As you can see here, it recovered 9 flashcards directly from the database. We just saved 40% of our output token costs on this document alone."
(Acción: Cambia a DBeaver/Cliente SQL y muestra la consulta con los dos deck_id distintos)
    "But in an Enterprise environment, sharing compute often implies a risk of cross-tenant data leakage. How do we prove this is secure?
    Look at this SQL query. We are querying the exact chunk_id that LanceDB matched. You can see that the exact same encrypted flashcards are now linked to two completely different deck_ids. We reused the encrypted data at rest.
    This is the core of the Frugal Fortress: Cross-document compute reuse with strict, cryptographic data isolation. The expensive AI work happens once, but tenancy remains absolute."
**(Action: In `config/runtime_tunable.json`, set `semantic_chunk_max_chars` back to 4000 and save.)**
## Act 5: DSPy, Token Trimming & The Nested Learning Loop (6:30 - 8:30)
(Action: Show the Arize Phoenix trace for a Premium user generation. Filter by span_kind == 'LLM' and click on the pdf_anki.llm.vertex_multimodal_pdf span.)
(Visual: Open docs/architecture/dspy_pilots.md and docs/architecture/NESTED_LEARNING_GUIDE.md, then show the sanitized Phoenix attributes and the compiled prompt policy in the IDE)
TÚ DICES:
"Prompt engineering is brittle. Instead, as documented in our DSPy Pilots guide, we use DSPy to compile heuristic policies offline.
If we look at this trace in Arize Phoenix for our Premium user, I want you to notice something critical for Enterprise compliance: The Input and Output prompts are completely hidden.
Because of our SOC2 Zero-Trust posture, our OpenTelemetry configuration strips all raw text before it hits our observability stack. However, we still retain full operational visibility. Here in the attributes (pdf_anki.llm.vertex_multimodal_pdf), you can see the pdf_anki.pipeline is 'unified', the user_tier is 'premium', and the exact token usage and latency.
But how do we know what instructions the AI received if we can't see the prompt?"
**(Action: Switch to your IDE and open config/dspy/pdf_anki.json. Highlight the unified_system_instruction_by_tier -> premium block.)**
TÚ DICES:
"We look at our compiled DSPy artifacts. Here is the exact JSON policy loaded into memory for this trace. You can see the statically injected instruction for Premium users: 'Bias toward concise atomic cards over broad summary cards.' This was compiled offline by DSPy to optimize token usage and study quality."
(Action: Switch back to the IDE, open src/app/modules/pdf_anki/application/token_optimizer.py and highlight the strip_headers_footers function.)
TÚ DICES:
"Furthermore, before any text hits the LLM, our strip_headers_footers Python function cleans up repetitive page numbers using pure regex. Saving 20 tokens per page on a 500-page document translates to massive FinOps savings at scale, completely independent of the AI model we use.
But we strictly separate these static heuristics from our real-time dynamic memory (RAG). Let me show you."
(Action: In the Next.js UI, open a previously generated deck. Find a flashcard that is too long or poorly formatted. Click "Edit", change the text to be extremely short and bulleted, and click "Save". Delete another flashcard and keep in mind what was talking about, to see if they reproduce it again or not later)
TÚ DICES:
"Instead of hardcoding rules, we built a 'Nested Learning Loop'. We are discarding this flashcard about """ and edit this other flashcard removing this scientific name for ATP. Let's look at what happened under the hood."
(Visual: Switch to Supabase SQL Editor. Run the safe SQL query provided:
SELECT
    id,
    CONCAT(SUBSTRING(tenant_id, 1, 4), '***') AS masked_tenant_id,
    status,
    score,
    user_prompt AS encrypted_prompt,
    user_correction AS encrypted_correction,
    model_used,
    SUBSTRING(embedding::text, 1, 40) || '...]' AS embedding_preview,
    created_at
FROM feedback_log
WHERE model_used LIKE 'pdf_anki_flashcard_feedback_%'
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 2;
TÚ DICES:
"When I clicked save, the backend vectorized the original card and my correction, and saved it to our PostgreSQL feedback_log table using pgvector.
Notice two things here: First, the status is APPROVED and the score is 1.0. The system has memorized my study preference. The other feedback about the discarded flashcard has a 0 score and REJECTED status. Second, look at the encrypted_prompt and encrypted_correction columns. They are Fernet tokens. Because of our Application-Level Encryption, even a DBA cannot read my flashcards. The data is secure at rest."
(Action:
Borra de pdf_anki_documents el documento recien subido (doc1).
Reinicia Redis, worker y api desde powershell: docker compose rm -sf redis; docker compose up -d redis; docker compose restart api worker
Lanza de nuevo Arize.
Go back to the UI.
Upload the doc1 again.
TÚ DICES:
"Now, after resetting Redis and removing the reference to this document, I'm uploading it again. Since our prompts are hidden in Phoenix for SOC2 compliance, how do we prove the AI is actually learning?"
(Visual: Switch to Grafana / Loki. Run the query {job="app"} |= "pdf_anki_few_shot_injected")
TÚ DICES:
"We look at our structured logs. Here in Loki, we see the event pdf_anki_few_shot_injected.
Before the LLM was invoked, our Adaptive Router performed a vector search in Postgres, found my previous correction, decrypted it in memory, and dynamically injected it into the System Prompt as a Few-Shot example.
(Action: Switch back to the UI and show the newly generated cards)
TÚ DICES:
"And if we look at the newly generated deck, the card talking about adenosine triphosphate (ATP) just shorts this term as ATP using the acronym. The system adapted to my personal pedagogical style in real-time, at zero training cost, and without ever exposing my raw data to our monitoring tools."
## Act 6: FinOps Sovereignty & Graceful Degradation (8:00 - 10:00)
(Action: Upload a 10-page PDF using a PAYG user whose balance is $0.00001, requesting a paid model like gemini-2.5-pro or flash-lite.)
TÚ DICES:
"Enterprise systems require strict FinOps. If you look at third-party observability UIs, they often estimate costs using generic, static pricing tables. But in an Enterprise environment, we cannot rely on frontend estimations for billing.
I just uploaded a 10-page document using a Pay-As-You-Go user who requested a premium Gemini model, but their wallet is practically empty."
(Visual: Switch to Grafana / Loki. Show the query {job="app"} |= "pdf_anki_provider_cost_estimate".)
TÚ DICES:
"Look at our Loki logs. Our backend maintains Financial Sovereignty. Before a single byte is sent to the AI, we calculate the exact provider cost down to the micro-cent based on our specific cloud agreements, and apply our markup."
(Visual: In Loki, change the query to {job="app"} |= "pdf_anki_payg_balance_insufficient_legacy_fallback".)
TÚ DICES:
"But what about Denial of Wallet attacks? If a user requests an expensive model without funds, our BudgetManager intercepts it.
Look at this log: pdf_anki_payg_balance_insufficient_legacy_fallback. The system saw the estimated charge exceeded the balance. Instead of crashing with a 500 error, it blocked the expensive model and gracefully degraded the user to the free-tier parsing stack."
(Visual: Switch to Arize Phoenix. Show the trace for this upload. Highlight that the spans say groq_flashcards instead of vertex, and point out there are only 3 LLM calls.)
TÚ DICES:
"If we look at the actual execution trace in Arize Phoenix, we can prove this. The spans clearly show the system routed the request to Groq's free tier instead of Vertex AI. We saved the user's experience and protected the company's profit margin simultaneously.
But notice something else in this trace. This is a 10-page document, which should require around 10 LLM calls. Yet, the trace stops after only 3 calls. Why?"
(Visual: Switch back to Grafana / Loki. Show the query {job="app"} |= "pdf_anki_flashcard_limit_reached", and then {job="app"} |= "completed_partial".)
TÚ DICES:
"Because the user was downgraded to the Free tier, they became subject to the Free tier limits—in this case, a maximum of 20 flashcards.
As our logs show, the moment the worker hit 20 cards on chunk index 2, it emitted a flashcard_limit_reached event and broke the loop. The database state is marked as completed_partial. We don't waste a single token generating cards we are just going to throw away. This is what true FinOps architecture looks like."
## Act 7: SRE (Site Reliability Engineering) & Async Burst Ingestion (10:00 - 11:30)
**(Action: In the Next.js UI, upload the 5 heavy PDFs in rapid succession. Use a mix of dense and long documents, USER PRO. and OPEN DEV TOOLS to show the 202 responses)**
**(Visual: Open `docs/diagrams/modules/pdf_anki/diagram_frontend_polling.md` to show the Concurrent Polling & Telemetry sequence)**
"What happens during a traffic spike? If five users upload heavy corporate PDFs at the exact same time, a naive API would block the event loop, and the database would crash under connection starvation.
But as you just saw, our API returned five HTTP 202 Accepted responses in milliseconds. The user is never blocked. As illustrated in our **Frontend Polling & Telemetry diagram**, the Next.js client seamlessly orchestrates concurrent polling for each background task without freezing the React render cycle. Furthermore, it tracks active viewport engagement to correlate LLM costs with actual user attention.
Let's look at our SRE dashboard to see how the system absorbs this impact.
**(Visual: Immediately switch to your browser tab with Grafana. Open the "Frugal Fortress - SRE Overview" dashboard. )**
**(DIRECTOR's NOTE FOR POST-PRODUCTION: Start a fast-forward / time-lapse effect here (e.g., 10x speed). Add a small "Fast Forward" icon overlay on the video so the viewer knows time is passing. Let the dashboard draw the metrics for the 2-3 minutes it takes to process the heavy PDFs, compressed into ~10-15 seconds of video.)**
**(Action: Point your cursor to the Blue line in the legend: `DB Connections (fastapi-app)`)**
Look at the blue line representing our FastAPI Gateway. It barely registers a blip. It accepted the 5 payloads, enqueued them, and freed the database connection instantly.
**(Action: Point your cursor to the Orange line: `DB Connections (arq-worker)`)**
Now look at the orange line. This is our asynchronous Arq worker pool. It instantly scales up to 5 active database connections, picking up all the heavy multimodal processing in the background. The API remains perfectly stable and ready to serve thousands of other users while the workers do the heavy lifting.
(Optional, adapt to the new pdfs experience)
**(Action: Switch to the Next.js UI to show the generated decks and their card counts.)**
And there is one more detail about our AI pipeline. Notice the results: a 10-page document - The constitution of the united states - generated X flashcards, while a X page book generated only x.
This is intentional. Our Unified Multimodal pipeline doesn't blindly generate 'X cards per page'. It evaluates the semantic density of the document. It extracts dense facts from cheat sheets, and ignores the corporate fluff in long manuals. We optimize for high-signal knowledge, saving output tokens and protecting the user's study time."
## Act 8: Security, GDPR & Data Lifecycle (11:30 - 12:30)
**(Visual: Open `docs/architecture/MULTI_TENANCY_AND_ISOLATION.md`)**
**Multi-Tenancy & Data Isolation Strategy**
"For Fortune 500 clients, data isolation and GDPR compliance are non-negotiable.
**(Visual `src/app/middleware/context.py` in the IDE, Highlight lines  where AppContext.set_tenant_id happens)**
First, we use Python `contextvars` via our `ContextMiddleware` to bind the `tenant_id` to every request and background job. As outlined in our , cross-tenant data leakage is mathematically impossible at the query level.
**(Visual Switch to docs/architecture/SECURITY_AND_COMPLIANCE_SOC2.md. Highlight the "Application-Level Encryption (ALE)" section)**
But we go a step further for SOC2 compliance. We implement Application-Level Encryption, meaning sensitive data is encrypted before it leaves the Python application.
**(Visual `SELECT * FROM pdf_flashcards LIMIT 5;` on Supabase )**
If we look at the production database, you can see the generated flashcards are encrypted at rest. Even a Database Administrator with full access cannot read the users' study material in clear text. The decryption only happens in memory during authorized API requests.
**(Visual 5: In Supabase, switch to a query showing the count of cards for a canceled user (the PRO user used on the last act, set current_period_end='2026-01-15T10:00:00+00:00' and subscription_status=canceled)**
SELECT count(*)
FROM pdf_flashcards
WHERE deck_id IN (
    SELECT id
    FROM pdf_decks
    WHERE user_id = (
        SELECT id
        FROM pdf_anki_users
        WHERE subscription_status = 'canceled'
        LIMIT 1
    )
);
Second, GDPR compliance is fully automated. If a user cancels their subscription, we give them a 90-day grace period. But what happens on day 91?
Here we have a user who canceled their subscription 4 months ago. They still have all these flashcards in the database. Let's trigger our daily Data Lifecycle cron job."
**(Action: Switch to your terminal and run `uv run python scripts/kera_force_prune.py`)**
**(Visual 6: Switch to Grafana / Loki and run the query `{job="app"} |= "pdf_anki_prune_completed"`)**
"The worker just woke up, detected the expired grace period, and executed a massive FIFO truncation. It deleted the old decks from PostgreSQL and purged the associated embeddings from our LanceDB vector store to comply with data minimization laws.
**(Action: Switch back to Supabase and re-run the `SELECT count(*)` query. The number should drop from 500 to 100)**
If we query the database again, the user's data has been hard-deleted down to the 100-card limit of the Free tier. Privacy is built into the architecture, requiring zero manual intervention from our engineering team."
## Act 9: Advanced QA Maturity & The Call to Action (12:30 - 14:00)
**(Visual: Switch from the IDE to your browser. Show the GitHub Actions UI with a green pipeline, specifically highlighting the "Mutation Testing (Core Logic)" job. Open `docs/operations/CI_CD_DEPLOYMENT_STRATEGY.md`. Then briefly flash the `reports/coverage_html/index.html` showing your ~90%+ coverage.)**
"Finally, how do we guarantee this architecture doesn't break when new engineers join the team? We prove our reliability through rigorous QA, orchestrated by our **Zero-Compromise CI/CD Pipeline**.
As you can see in our coverage report, we maintain a strict 90%+ baseline. But standard code coverage is a vanity metric; it tells you a line was executed, not that it was tested correctly.
That's why, in our CI/CD pipeline, we use Mutation Testing (`mutmut`) to actively modify the Abstract Syntax Tree during the build process. We don't waste CI compute mutating external API wrappers; we focus this exclusively on our Core domain.
**(Visual: Open `docs/testing/MUTATION_PRAGMAS.md` and `ADR-0005` in the IDE)**
As explained in our **Mutation Pragmas** doc and **ADR-0005** regarding Async Test Scoping, we empirically prove that our FinOps token counting, our semantic chunking logic, and our security guardrails are unbreakable. If a test doesn't catch a mutation in the core, the build fails and the deployment is halted.
This is Kera: secure, auditable, and economically predictable. I would be happy to dive deeper into the codebase, our infrastructure setup, or discuss how these patterns can scale your current operations during a technical review. Thank you."
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/DEMO docs/script_elevator_pitch.md`
```md
# docs/pdf-anki/DEMO docs/script_elevator_pitch.md
# The Elevator Pitch: PDF to Anki (Video 1 Script)
*Target Audience: Recruiters, Hiring Managers, Non-Technical Stakeholders.*
*Tone: High-energy, problem-solving, business-value focused.*
*Duration: ~2.5 Minutes.*
---
## Act 1: The Hook & The Problem (0:00 - 0:45)
**(Visual: Start with your face on camera, then transition to a clean screen showing the Next.js Frontend on the left, and a simple dashboard/Grafana on the right)**
"Processing massive, 500-page corporate PDFs usually results in two things: crashing APIs and skyrocketing cloud bills. If you naively send an entire textbook to an AI model, you are wasting thousands of dollars on redundant tokens.
I'm Javier, and today I'll show you the PDF to Anki module of the 'Frugal Fortress'. It's an enterprise-grade asynchronous pipeline I engineered to ingest massive documents and generate spaced-repetition flashcards, operating with a baseline infrastructure cost of just $5 a month. Let's see it."
## Act 2: The "Wow" Factor - Async Ingestion & L2 Cache (0:45 - 1:30)
**(Action: In the Next.js UI, upload a 50-page PDF. The UI instantly shows 'Processing'.)**
"Notice the speed. The frontend never blocks the user. Uploading returns an HTTP 202 Accepted instantly, and our background workers take over.
But here is where the architecture shines. Watch what happens when I upload a document with repetitive concepts."
**(Action: Set chunk limit to 500 characters. (may need to execute 'uv run python scripts/lancedb_drop_pdf_anki_chunks.py'. Upload `doc_v1.pdf`. Wait for it to complete. Then upload `doc_v2.pdf` which contains the same text plus one new paragraph.)**
**(Visual: Point to the Grafana dashboard showing the Loki log `{job="app"} |= "semantic_cache_hit_skipping_llm"` and Cost: $0.00 for that chunk)**
"The system didn't just blindly process the whole file. I implemented a Semantic Deduplication layer using LanceDB. The worker chunks the text, embeds it, and checks the vector database. If a concept is 95% similar to something we already processed, we skip the LLM generation entirely. We save massive amounts of output tokens on repetitive textbooks."
## Act 3: The "Nested Learning Loop" (Self-Correcting AI) (1:30 - 2:15)
**(Action: In the Next.js UI, open a generated deck (doc1). Edit a flashcard to make it shorter or fix a term, and click "Save". Delete another flashcard and keep it in mind to see if it reproduces the same one again.)**
"Off-the-shelf AI models make mistakes or generate bloated answers. But fine-tuning models to fix this is incredibly slow and expensive.
Instead, I built a 'Nested Learning Loop'. When a user edits a flashcard to match their personal study style, the backend instantly vectorizes that correction and saves it to PostgreSQL using pgvector.
ACTION (VIDEO CUT):
Borra de pdf_anki_documents el documento recien subido (doc1).
Reinicia Redis, worker y api desde powershell: docker compose rm -sf redis; docker compose up -d redis; docker compose restart api worker
Lanza de nuevo Arize.
Go back to the UI.
Upload the doc1 again. Analiza las tarjetas y encuentra la similitud con tu corrección.
(Visual: Switch to Grafana / Loki. Run the query {job="app"} |= "pdf_anki_few_shot_injected")
TÚ DICES:
The next time they upload a document, the system dynamically retrieves their past edits and injects them into the AI's context. The system learns the user's exact pedagogical style in real-time, at zero training cost. The more they use it, the smarter it gets."
## Act 4: The Call to Action (2:15 - 2:30)
**(Visual: Back to your face on camera, with logos of FastAPI, LanceDB, Postgres, GitHub Actions, and SOC2 floating next to you)**
"This system doesn't just work; it scales securely. It features a Pay-As-You-Go wallet with Graceful Degradation to prevent Denial of Wallet attacks, Application-Level Encryption for SOC2 compliance, and a CI/CD pipeline with Mutation Testing to guarantee reliability.
If you want to see the Hexagonal Architecture, the LanceDB vector search, and the rigorous QA guardrails behind this, check out the 10-minute Architecture Deep Dive video below. Thanks for watching."
```

### `docs/pdf-anki/DEMO docs/observabilidad.md`
```md
Escenario 2.0: El Choque de Pipelines (Chunked vs Massive Multimodal)
    Acción A (Free): Sube un PDF de 5 páginas con un usuario free.
    Acción B (Pro/PAYG): Sube un libro o manual técnico de 100 páginas con un usuario pro (Vertex AI).
    Dónde mirar: Arize Phoenix.
    El Flex:
        Abre la traza Free: Mostrarás un árbol con múltiples spans hijos (una llamada a Groq por cada párrafo/chunk). Explica que esto minimiza el uso de memoria pero aumenta la latencia.
        Abre la traza Pro: Mostrarás una única traza masiva sin spans hijos. El Input tendrá cientos de miles de tokens (los bytes del PDF en Base64). Explica que Vertex AI procesa el documento entero de un solo golpe, manteniendo la cohesión del contexto y garantizando Zero Data Retention.
Escenario 2.1: Deduplicación Semántica (L2 Cache)
    Acción: Crea un PDF (test_dedup.pdf) que contenga 3 párrafos, donde el párrafo 1 y el párrafo 3 sean exactamente el mismo texto. Súbelo con un usuario Free.
    Dónde mirar: Grafana (Loki) (Logs del Worker).
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        Busca en Loki: {job="app"} |= "semantic_cache_hit_skipping_llm".
        Verás que el worker procesó el chunk 1 y 2, pero al llegar al chunk 3, detectó una similitud > 95% en LanceDB y se saltó la llamada a Groq, ahorrando tokens de salida.
Escenario 2.2: DSPy vs RAG (Instrucciones Optimizadas)
Añadir en .env:
DSPY_ENABLED=true
DSPY_PDF_ANKI_ENABLED=true
    Acción: Sube un PDF utilizando un usuario con Tier premium (constitucion 1, descartas una flashcard y editas y guardas otra, luego borras el deck con intención de subir el PDF de nuevo).
    Dónde mirar: Arize Phoenix.
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        Abre la traza de la generación de flashcards.
        Revisa el System Prompt. Debes ver la instrucción inyectada estáticamente por DSPy: "Bias toward concise atomic cards over broad summary cards.", y tambien los ejemplos few-shot inyectados dinámicamente a partir de las correcciones anteriores.
        Esto demuestra que tu sistema separa la heurística compilada offline (DSPy) de la memoria dinámica en tiempo real (RAG).
Escenario 2.3: Partial Completion (Límites de Tier)
    Acción: Sube un PDF de 10 paginas (constitucion) usando un usuario free (cuyo límite es, por ejemplo, 40 flashcards).
    Dónde mirar: Grafana (Loki) y Arize Phoenix.
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        En Grafana (Loki) (Logs del Worker): Busca el log "event": "pdf_anki_flashcard_limit_reached". Verifica que el estado final del documento en base de datos se guardó como completed_partial.
        En Arize Phoenix: Verás que las llamadas al LLM se detienen abruptamente al alcanzar el límite de cartas. No hay llamadas "desperdiciadas" que luego se descarten en código; el bucle se rompe eficientemente.
Escenario 2.4: Token Trimming (Optimización de Contexto)
    Acción: Sube un PDF que tenga números de página y cabeceras repetitivas ("Página 1 de 50", "Borrador Confidencial") en cada página.
    Dónde mirar: Arize Phoenix (comparando el PDF original con el Input del LLM).
    El Flex: Muestra cómo la función strip_headers_footers limpió la basura antes de enviarla al LLM. Explica que ahorrar 20 tokens por página en un documento de 500 páginas se traduce en un ahorro masivo a escala.
2.5 Graceful Degradation (FinOps Fallback)
    Acción: Configura un usuario con Tier payg y pon su balance_usd a $0.000001 en la base de datos. Intenta procesar un PDF seleccionando el modelo gemini-2.5-pro.
    Dónde mirar: Grafana (Loki) y Arize Phoenix.
    Qué verificar (El Flex):
        En Grafana (Loki): Busca el log "event": "pdf_anki_payg_balance_insufficient_fallback". Esto demuestra la decisión de negocio: el BudgetManager bloqueó el uso del modelo caro por falta de fondos.
        En Arize Phoenix: Verifica que la traza resultante se ejecutó utilizando el modelo de fallback (ej. groq/llama-3.3-70b-versatile) y el coste de la operación, salvando la experiencia del usuario.
Escenario 2.6: Async Burst Ingestion & Worker Backpressure
    Acción: Sube 5 PDFs pesados en rápida sucesión (puedes usar un script rápido o darle click muy rápido en la UI).
    Dónde mirar: Grafana (Dashboards de Prometheus y Loki).
    El Flex:
        Muestra que la API devolvió 5 respuestas HTTP 202 Accepted en milisegundos (el usuario nunca se queda bloqueado).
        Muestra en Grafana cómo la métrica background_tasks_active sube.
        Muestra cómo el CpuBoundExecutor y el pool de conexiones de Postgres (db_pool_checked_out) se mantienen estables sin colapsar, procesando la cola de Arq de forma ordenada. "El sistema absorbe el pico de tráfico asíncronamente protegiendo la base de datos".
```
